title: SAE-Guided Diffusion: A Research Exploration
subtitle: Living document tracking our investigation into combining sparse autoencoders with diffusion models for controllable text generation

children.title: What This Is

children: This is a
living research document
tracking our investigation into combining two powerful ideas:
sparse autoencoders (SAEs)
for interpretable feature extraction, and
diffusion models
for text generation. We're exploring whether SAE features can serve as a structured, interpretable latent space for diffusion-based text generation.

The field of text diffusion is moving fast. As of early 2026, models like LLaDA2.1 (100B with T2T editing, 892 TPS), Dream 7B, and Gemini Diffusion (1,479 tok/sec) have proven that discrete diffusion scales to frontier quality. Meanwhile, SAE-based steering of language models is maturing rapidly, with DLM-Scope (Feb 2026) showing that SAEs work BETTER on diffusion LMs than AR LLMs — SAE insertion can actually REDUCE loss in DLMs. The question we're asking:
can these two threads be woven together?

children.title: The Big Picture

children: Imagine you're a sculptor. An autoregressive model carves left-to-right, one chip at a time, never going back. A diffusion model starts with a rough block and refines the whole piece simultaneously. SAE features are like a catalog of known shapes—'nose', 'hand', 'flowing fabric'—that the sculptor can reference. Our question: can we make the diffusion sculptor work by composing from this catalog instead of raw marble?

children.title: Idea 1: Structured Latent Spaces

children: Core hypothesis:

Use SAE feature activations as the diffusion latent space. Instead of diffusing over token embeddings or discrete tokens, diffuse over sparse, interpretable SAE feature vectors. Each dimension has a known meaning.

children.title: [Feb 13] Latent Diffusion EBM for Interpretable Text (ICML 2022)

children: Paper:
Yu et al., 'Latent Diffusion Energy-Based Model for Interpretable Text Modeling' (ICML 2022, arXiv:2206.05895)
Key insight:

They combine diffusion with energy-based models in a variational framework for text, using geometric clustering-based regularization + information bottleneck to create interpretable latent spaces. This is the closest existing work to our Idea 1.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT. They prove that structured latent spaces improve both generation quality AND interpretability in text diffusion. Their geometric clustering is analogous to what SAE features naturally provide (sparse, grouped representations). The key difference: they learn their structured space; we'd use pre-trained SAE features as the structure.
Open question:

Their latent space is continuous and relatively low-dimensional. SAE feature spaces are very high-dimensional (16K-128K features) but sparse. Is high-dimensional-but-sparse better or worse for diffusion?

children.title: [Feb 16] LaDiR: Latent Diffusion Reasoner — Structured Latent Space for Text Reasoning (Oct 2025)

children: Paper:
Kang et al., 'LaDiR: Latent Diffusion Enhances LLMs for Text Reasoning' (arXiv:2510.04573, Oct 2025, updated Dec 2025)
Key insight:

Constructs a structured latent reasoning space using a VAE that encodes text reasoning steps into blocks of thought tokens. A latent diffusion model then denoises these blocks with blockwise bidirectional attention, enabling iterative refinement and parallel generation of diverse reasoning trajectories. Outperforms AR, diffusion-based, and latent reasoning baselines on math reasoning and planning.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT for Idea 1. LaDiR proves that diffusing in a structured latent space (VAE-encoded reasoning steps) beats diffusing over raw tokens for text. Their VAE creates compact, semantic representations — exactly analogous to what SAE features provide. Key difference: they learn the latent space via VAE training, while we'd use pre-trained SAE features as an already-interpretable structured space. Their success validates the core premise: structured latent spaces improve text diffusion quality.
New research question:

Could SAE features replace the VAE encoder in LaDiR's architecture? SAE features are already interpretable (no training needed), sparse (efficient), and semantically meaningful. The LaDiR framework + SAE feature space could be a direct implementation path for Idea 1.

children.title: [Feb 13] DiffuLLaMA: Adapting AR Models to Diffusion (2024-25)

children: Paper:
Gong et al., 'Scaling Diffusion Language Models via Adaptation from Autoregressive Models' (arXiv:2410.17891)
Key insight:

You can convert pretrained AR models (GPT-2, LLaMA) into diffusion models via continual pre-training with <200B tokens. They create DiffuGPT (127M-355M) and DiffuLLaMA (7B), showing competitive performance. Critical detail: they unify AR and diffusion objectives, showing they're more similar than assumed.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ PRACTICAL ENABLER. If we can convert an AR model to a diffusion model, and we already have SAEs trained on that AR model's activations, we might be able to reuse those SAEs in the diffusion setting. The feature space from GPT-2 SAEs could potentially serve as the structured latent space for DiffuGPT.

children.title: [Feb 14] 🔥🔥🔥 DLM-Scope: First SAE Framework for Diffusion LMs (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Wang et al., 'DLM-Scope: Mechanistic Interpretability of Diffusion Language Models via Sparse Autoencoders' (arXiv:2602.05859, Feb 5, 2026, HKU/Alibaba)
Key insight:

First SAE-based interpretability framework specifically for diffusion language models. Trains Top-K SAEs on Dream-7B and LLaDA-8B. CRITICAL FINDING: SAE insertion affects DLMs DIFFERENTLY than LLMs — while SAE insertion in LLMs typically incurs a loss penalty, in DLMs it can REDUCE cross-entropy loss when applied to early layers!
Other findings:

(1) SAE features in DLMs enable MORE effective diffusion-time interventions, often outperforming LLM steering. (2) SAEs can provide useful signals for DLM decoding order. (3) SAE features are stable during post-training (SFT) phase of DLMs.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ GAME-CHANGER for our project. This is direct validation that SAEs work on diffusion LMs AND work BETTER than on AR LLMs for steering. The early-layer loss reduction suggests DLMs have a natural affinity for SAE-structured representations. Our Idea 1 (structured latent spaces) gets strong support — if SAEs reduce loss when inserted, the SAE feature space may be a MORE natural space for DLM computation than raw activations.
New research question:
Why does SAE insertion reduce loss in DLMs but increase it in LLMs? Is this because diffusion's iterative refinement naturally benefits from sparse, disentangled representations?

children.title: [Feb 14] GLP: Diffusion Models as Meta-Models of LLM Activations (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Luo, Feng, Darrell, Radford, Steinhardt (UC Berkeley). 'Learning a Generative Meta-Model of LLM Activations' (arXiv:2602.06964, Feb 6, 2026)
Key insight:

Trains diffusion models on ONE BILLION residual stream activations to create 'meta-models' that learn the distribution of a network's internal states. Critical finding: applying the meta-model's learned prior to steering interventions IMPROVES FLUENCY by projecting off-manifold activations back onto the natural activation manifold.
Scaling behavior:
Diffusion loss decreases smoothly with compute and reliably predicts downstream utility. Meta-model neurons increasingly isolate concepts into individual units (like SAEs do).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ ALTERNATIVE APPROACH to our work. While we propose using SAE feature space for diffusion, GLP proposes using diffusion TO MODEL activation space. These are complementary: GLP shows that diffusion naturally learns to represent the structure of activations; we propose using that structure (via SAEs) as the diffusion target. Key implication: GLP's on-manifold projection could be combined with SAE steering for better fluency.
New research question:
Could we train a diffusion model in SAE feature space (our Idea 1) and use GLP-style manifold projection to ensure generated features stay on the valid SAE manifold?

children.title: [Feb 14] LoMDM: Learnable Generation Orderings (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Jong Chul Ye et al. (KAIST/KRAFTON), 'Unifying Masked Diffusion Models with Various Generation Orders and Beyond' (arXiv:2602.02112, Feb 3, 2026)
Key insight:

Introduces 'learnable-order masked diffusion model' (LoMDM) that JOINTLY learns generation ordering and diffusion backbone through a single objective from scratch. This enables context-dependent ordering where different inputs can have different optimal generation orders.
Framework:
OeMDM (order-expressive MDM) provides a unified framework interpreting MDM, ARM, and block diffusion in a single formulation.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT for Idea 3. If generation order can be learned, we could learn an SAE-feature-aware ordering: denoise high-level semantic features first, low-level syntactic features last. LoMDM proves the ordering is learnable; we'd add the constraint that ordering should follow semantic hierarchy.

children.title: [Feb 13 7pm] RouteSAE: Multi-Layer Feature Routing for Structured Interpretability (EMNLP 2025)

children: Paper:
Shi, Li, Liang, Wan, Ma, Wang, He (USTC/Douyin). 'Route Sparse Autoencoder to Interpret Large Language Models' (arXiv:2503.08200, EMNLP 2025)
Key insight:

Introduces a routing mechanism with a shared SAE to extract features across MULTIPLE layers simultaneously, rather than one layer at a time. Dynamically assigns weights to activations from different layers with minimal parameter overhead. Under sparsity constraint of 64, extracts 22.5% more features than baseline SAEs with 22.3% higher interpretability.
Critical finding for our work:

Low-level features (word-level polysemy disambiguation like 'crushed things', 'Europe') peak in EARLY layers and decline in deeper layers. High-level features (sentence structure like 'more [X] than [Y]', 'enumeration') show INCREASING activation with depth. This multi-layer activation pattern means single-layer SAEs miss features that span the hierarchy.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT for Ideas 1 & 3. For Idea 1 (structured latent space): RouteSAE proves that multi-layer routing captures a RICHER feature set than single-layer SAEs — exactly what we'd want for a diffusion latent space. For Idea 3 (semantic noise schedules): the low-level-early/high-level-late pattern is quantified across layers, giving us a concrete blueprint for which features to noise at which schedule stages. Also relevant to polysemy: if SAE features can disambiguate word senses across layers, the structured latent space naturally handles lexical ambiguity during generation.

children.title: [Feb 15] SAEs + ITDA on Flux 1: Dictionary Learning Scales to Large Diffusion Models (CVPR 2025)

children: Paper:
'Interpreting Large Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with Dictionary Learning' — applies SAEs and Inference-Time Decomposition of Activations (ITDA) to Flux 1.
Key insight:

SAEs accurately reconstruct residual stream embeddings in large DiTs and beat MLP neurons on interpretability. ITDA (using data points as dictionary elements, reconstructed via gradient pursuit) achieves comparable interpretability without training.
Steering works:
SAE features can steer image generation through activation addition — confirming that sparse dictionary features are causally meaningful in large diffusion models.
Relevance:

Strengthens Idea 1: if SAE features form a good structured space for image diffusion, the same should hold for text diffusion latent spaces. The ITDA finding is interesting — could we use ITDA instead of trained SAEs for dictionary-guided denoising (Idea 4)?
Source:
arXiv:2505.24360, CVPR 2025 Workshop on Mechanistic Interpretability for Vision

children.title: [Feb 17] 🔥🔥 CADD: Continuous Latents Augmenting Discrete Diffusion (Apple/Microsoft, Oct 2025)

children: Paper:
Zheng et al., 'Continuously Augmented Discrete Diffusion model for Categorical Generative Modeling' (arXiv:2510.01329, Oct 2025, Apple/Microsoft)
Key insight:

Standard discrete diffusion maps unobserved tokens to [MASK], creating an 'information void' where semantic context is lost. CADD augments the discrete state space with a PAIRED DIFFUSION in continuous latent space — masked tokens are represented by noisy but informative latent vectors instead of collapsed voids. At each reverse step, the continuous latent serves as a 'semantic hint' to guide discrete denoising.
Critical feature:

At sampling time, the strength of the continuous latent estimator enables a controlled trade-off between mode-coverage (diverse) and mode-seeking (precise) generation. Improves over mask-based diffusion on text, image, AND code.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT ARCHITECTURE FOR IDEA 1! CADD's continuous latent space augmenting discrete diffusion is EXACTLY what we'd build with SAE features. Replace CADD's generic continuous latents with SAE feature activations → masked tokens get 'semantic hint' vectors that are INTERPRETABLE. The mode-coverage/mode-seeking trade-off maps to controlling how strongly SAE features constrain generation. Together with CCDD (which jointly diffuses in continuous+discrete), CADD provides a second proven architecture for our continuous-SAE-guides-discrete-tokens approach.
Key advantage over CCDD:
CADD is compatible with existing discrete diffusion training — minimal architectural change needed. This makes it a more practical starting point for SAE integration experiments.

children.title: [Feb 17] 🔥🔥 Mechanism Shift: Why DLMs Reorganize Internal Computation (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Kong et al., 'Mechanism Shift During Post-training from Autoregressive to Masked Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2601.14758, Jan 21, 2026)
Key insight:

Comparative circuit analysis of ARMs vs post-trained MDMs reveals a systematic 'mechanism shift': (1) For tasks with local causal dependencies, MDMs retain autoregressive circuitry. (2) For GLOBAL PLANNING tasks, MDMs completely abandon AR pathways, exhibiting distinct rewiring with INCREASED EARLY-LAYER PROCESSING. (3) Semantically, MDMs transition from sharp localized specialization to DISTRIBUTED INTEGRATION.
Why this matters for SAE+diffusion:

⭐⭐⭐ EXPLAINS DLM-SCOPE! The finding that MDMs shift to distributed integration (vs. localized specialization in ARMs) directly explains WHY SAE insertion reduces loss in DLMs but increases it in LLMs. SAEs decompose representations into distributed features — which aligns with MDMs' distributed computation. The increased early-layer processing in MDMs also explains why SAEs are most beneficial in early layers of DLMs (DLM-Scope's finding). This is mechanistic evidence that SAE-structured representations are NATURALLY COMPATIBLE with diffusion LM computation.
Implications for all ideas:

The distributed integration finding suggests that Idea 1 (SAE feature space as latent space) is especially promising for global planning tasks (reasoning, structured generation). The early-layer emphasis suggests our noise schedule (Idea 3) should prioritize early-layer SAE features.

children.title: [Feb 17] LASERS: Dictionary-Based Sparse Latent Space for Generative Models (Sep 2024)

children: Paper:
Li et al., 'LASERS: LAtent Space Encoding for Representations with Sparsity for Generative Modeling' (arXiv:2409.11184, Sep 2024)
Key insight:

Proposes replacing Vector Quantization (VQ) in VAEs/GANs with dictionary-based sparse representations under sparsity constraints. The dictionary is learned during training. Shows that sparse dictionary latent spaces are MORE EXPRESSIVE than VQ and produce better reconstructions, while also addressing codebook collapse. Key theoretical insight: the benefit of VQ may not be from discretization but from LOSSY COMPRESSION — and sparse dictionaries provide a better form of it.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ CONCEPTUAL PREDECESSOR to Idea 1. LASERS proves that dictionary-based sparse representations work as latent spaces for generative models (images). Our Idea 1 goes further: instead of learning a new dictionary during training, use pre-trained SAE features as the dictionary. The 'sparse > VQ for expressiveness' finding supports our intuition that high-dimensional-but-sparse SAE feature spaces should work well for generation. LASERS is for VAEs/GANs; we'd apply the same principle to diffusion.

children.title: 📡 Feb 18, 2026: Scaling Beyond Masked Diffusion (arXiv:2602.15014)

children: Sahoo et al. present the
first scaling law study
of uniform-state and interpolating discrete diffusion. Key surprise:
perplexity is misleading across diffusion families
. Uniform-state diffusion outperforms both autoregressive AND masked diffusion on GSM8K despite worse validation perplexity. Scaled to 1.7B params.

For our work: if uniform-state diffusion preserves different latent dynamics than masked diffusion, the choice of diffusion family matters for how SAE features interact with the latent space. Uniform-state may preserve more compositional structure.

children.title: Scaling Laws for Discrete Diffusion: Uniform vs Masked (Feb 2026)

children: Scaling Behavior of Discrete Diffusion

(von Rutte et al., updated Feb 15 2026, arXiv:2512.10858) provides the first comprehensive scaling law study of discrete DLMs across noise types (masked vs uniform interpolation). Key finding: uniform diffusion requires MORE parameters but LESS data for compute-efficient training than masked diffusion. Scaled to 10B parameters -- largest publicly known uniform diffusion model.
Relevance to Idea 1:

Critical for design choices. If we build SAE-guided diffusion in structured latent space, uniform diffusion may be better (less data-hungry, meaning our smaller SAE-feature datasets might suffice). But it requires larger models -- suggesting we might need efficient architectures (FS-DFM style) to keep SAE-guided uniform diffusion practical. Also confirms that Sahoo et al.'s finding (uniform outperforms on GSM8K despite worse perplexity) holds at scale.
New question:

Does the uniform-state vs masked-state distinction affect how SAE features interact with the diffusion process? Uniform noise preserves more structure than masking -- potentially better for SAE feature alignment.

children.title: [Feb 20] 🔥 FLM/FMLM: Continuous Flows Challenge Discrete Diffusion (Feb 18, 2026)

children: Paper:
Kim et al., 'One-step Language Modeling via Continuous Denoising' (arXiv:2602.16813, Feb 18, 2026)
Key insight:

Discrete diffusion language models degrade sharply in the few-step regime. FLM uses flow-based CONTINUOUS denoising over one-hot token encodings instead. A distilled version (FMLM) achieves one-step generation that EXCEEDS 8-step discrete diffusion quality. Challenges the assumption that discrete diffusion processes are necessary for discrete modalities. Code released.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ PARADIGM CHALLENGE for our project. If continuous flows beat discrete diffusion for text, our SAE-guided diffusion (Idea 1) should consider continuous flow-based denoising as the base instead of masked/uniform discrete diffusion. SAE features are inherently continuous-valued — they'd integrate MORE NATURALLY with continuous flows than with discrete masking. This could simplify our architecture: diffuse in continuous SAE feature space, decode to tokens via the flow map. Combined with CADD (continuous augmenting discrete), FLM suggests the field is moving toward continuous representations even for discrete data.
New question:

Can we train a flow-based model directly in SAE feature space? FLM denoises over one-hot encodings; we'd denoise over SAE feature activations. The continuous nature of both SAE features and flow matching could be a natural fit.

props.title: Feb 20, 2026: Uniform Diffusion Challenges Masked — Implications for SAE Latent Spaces

children: — First scaling law study across masked, uniform-state, and interpolating discrete diffusion. Key finding: uniform-state outperforms AR and masked on GSM8K at 1.7B params despite worse perplexity. Perplexity is misleading across families.

children.children: Why this matters for SAE integration:

children: Uniform-state diffusion uses full-vocabulary noise (not just [MASK] tokens). This means intermediate denoising states have richer token distributions — potentially much better for SAE feature decomposition. A masked state is just 'unknown'; a uniform-noise state is 'could be anything' with meaningful probability gradients. SAE features trained on normal forward passes would fire more meaningfully on uniform-noise states than on [MASK] tokens.

children.children: Companion paper (arXiv:2512.10858) scales uniform diffusion to 10B parameters, confirming these models are viable at scale. Uniform diffusion is also more data-efficient (more params, less data), relevant for prototype development.

children.title: [Feb 21] CaDDi: Non-Markovian Discrete Diffusion with Causal LM Weights (NeurIPS 2025)

children: Paper:
'Non-Markovian Discrete Diffusion with Causal Language Models' (NeurIPS 2025 poster)
Key idea:

CaDDi lifts the Markov constraint from discrete diffusion by conditioning on the entire generative trajectory, not just the current noised state. It unifies causal (sequential) and temporal (diffusion) reasoning in a single transformer, treating standard causal LMs as a special case. Crucially, it can directly reuse pretrained LLM weights with no architectural changes.
Relevance to Idea 1 (Structured Latent Spaces):

⭐⭐ CaDDi's trajectory conditioning means the model has access to the full history of denoising states — a richer 'latent' context than single-step Markov models. If SAE features were extracted at each step, the non-Markovian framework could condition on the evolution of feature activations across the trajectory, potentially enabling more coherent structured generation.
Relevance to Idea 4 (Dictionary-Guided Denoising):

⭐⭐ The trajectory-aware architecture is a natural fit for dictionary-guided denoising: at each step, SAE projections from all previous steps are visible, letting the model learn which feature corrections were effective and adjust accordingly. This could make iterative SAE projection more stable.
Practical note:
The ability to reuse pretrained AR LLM weights is significant — it means SAEs already trained on those LLMs could potentially transfer directly to the CaDDi diffusion process.

children.title: [Feb 24] DSL: Discrete Stochastic Localization for Self-Correcting DLMs (Feb 18, 2026)

children: Paper:
Wu et al., 'Discrete Stochastic Localization for Non-autoregressive Generation' (arXiv:2602.16169, Feb 18, 2026)
Key insight:

Trains a single SNR-invariant denoiser across a continuum of corruption levels, bridging draft noise and mask-style endpoint corruption within one Diffusion Transformer. Achieves ~4x fewer denoiser evaluations than MDLM+ReMDM baseline while matching quality. Shows improved self-correction and uncertainty calibration.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ PRACTICAL FOR IDEAS 3 & 4. The SNR-invariant denoiser operates across ALL corruption levels with a single model — meaning SAE feature guidance could be applied uniformly regardless of noise level, simplifying our dictionary-guided denoising (Idea 4). The self-correction capability is especially relevant: if SAE-guided corrections introduce small errors, DSL's self-correction mechanism could recover from them. The 4x step reduction also means SAE guidance per-step overhead is less costly.

children.title: [Feb 24] P2: Path Planning for MDM Sampling — Planning + Denoising Decomposition (ICLR 2026)

children: Paper:
'Path Planning for Diffusion Language Model Sampling' (OpenReview #11824, ICLR 2026 submission)
Key insight:

Decomposes each MDM generation step into two sub-stages: a PLANNER selects which tokens to update, and a DENOISER fills them in. Critically, this enables REFINING already-unmasked tokens — something standard MDMs cannot do. Proves an expanded ELBO on log-likelihood. Massive gains: +22% protein foldability, +68% story ROUGE, +33% code pass@1.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT FRAMEWORK FOR IDEAS 3 & 4. The planning-denoising decomposition is exactly where SAE features plug in: the PLANNER could use SAE feature analysis to decide which tokens need updating (those whose SAE features don't match target), and the DENOISER could be guided by SAE dictionary constraints. The token refinement capability enables iterative SAE-guided correction — unmask tokens, check their SAE features, remask/refine misaligned ones. Complements Where-to-Unmask and LoMDM with a more general framework.

children.title: [Feb 24] GRPO for MDM Unmasking: RL-Learned Scheduling with Guarantees (ICLR 2026)

children: Paper:
'Improving Discrete Diffusion Unmasking Policies Beyond Explicit Reference Policies' (OpenReview #5629, ICLR 2026 submission)
Key insight:

Casts MDM denoising as a KL-regularized MDP and uses GRPO to learn an optimal unmasking policy. Proves convergence guarantees — the learned policy generates samples closer to the data distribution than heuristic schedules. On Sudoku (where order is critical): +22% over random, +12% over max-confidence.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ COMPLEMENTS IDEA 3. Another approach to learned generation ordering (alongside LoMDM, Where-to-Unmask). The KL-regularized MDP formulation is theoretically clean and could be extended: add SAE feature matching as a reward signal → the RL policy learns to unmask in an order that maximizes SAE feature coherence. The convergence guarantees would carry over, giving theoretical backing to SAE-aware scheduling.

children.title: [Feb 23] STAR-LDM: Latent Diffusion Planning for Language Generation (COLM 2026 submission)

children: Paper:
'Stop-Think-AutoRegress: Language Modeling with Latent Diffusion Planning' (OpenReview, COLM 2026 submission)
Key insight:

Integrates latent diffusion planning with AR generation. The model pauses to refine a semantic plan through diffusion in continuous latent space before committing to discrete tokens. >70% win rate in narrative coherence. Lightweight classifiers enable fine-grained attribute steering without retraining.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT ARCHITECTURE FOR IDEA 1. STAR-LDM’s ‘think in continuous latent space, then commit to tokens’ is exactly what SAE-guided diffusion would do. Replace their generic latent space with SAE feature space → the ‘thinking phase’ becomes interpretable planning over known semantic features. Combined with LaDiR and CADD, a third proven architecture for continuous-latent-guides-discrete-tokens.

children.title: Idea 2: Feature Steering for Conditional Generation

children: Core hypothesis:

Activate specific SAE features to seed or steer diffusion-based generation. Instead of text prompts, specify desired features (e.g., 'formal tone' + 'scientific topic' + 'persuasive structure') and let the diffusion model generate text that exhibits those features.

children.title: [Feb 13] SAEs Are Good for Steering — If You Select the Right Features (May 2025)

children: Paper:
Arad, Mueller, Belinkov (Technion/Northeastern), arXiv:2505.20063
Key insight:

SAE features split into 'input features' (detect patterns in input) vs 'output features' (actually change model output when amplified). These rarely co-occur in the same features! Using output features for steering gives 2-3x improvement, making unsupervised SAE steering competitive with supervised methods like LoRA.
Critical finding:
Early layers have more input features (syntactic detectors), later layers have more output features (semantic drivers). This has direct implications for our noise schedule idea too (Idea 3).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT with IMPORTANT CAVEAT. SAE steering works well, but only if you pick output features. For our diffusion+SAE approach, we'd need to use output features as the conditioning signal, not input features. The input/output distinction may also inform which features to include in the latent space.

children.title: [Feb 13] Control RL: Token-Level Steering via SAE Features (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Cho et al., 'Control Reinforcement Learning' (arXiv:2602.10437, just published!)
Key insight:

Trains an RL policy to select which SAE features to amplify at each token position. Produces interpretable intervention logs. Key discovery: 'branch points' — specific tokens where the feature choice determines output correctness.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ STRONG SUPPORT. This is per-token SAE feature selection — essentially learned conditional generation through SAE features. The RL policy learns WHEN and WHERE to apply features, which is analogous to what a diffusion denoiser would need to learn: which features to 'solidify' at each denoising step.

children.title: [Feb 23] 🔥🔥 TADA!: SAEs Applied to Audio Diffusion Models for Steering (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Staniszewski et al., 'TADA! Tuning Audio Diffusion Models through Activation Steering' (arXiv:2602.11910, Feb 12, 2026)
Key insight:

First paper to apply Sparse Autoencoders directly inside diffusion models (audio domain). Uses activation patching to show that distinct semantic musical concepts (instruments, vocals, genre) are controlled by a small subset of attention layers. Then applies both Contrastive Activation Addition AND SAEs in those layers for precise steering — modulating tempo, changing mood, altering instrumentation.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ CRITICAL VALIDATION. This is the first demonstration of SAEs working INSIDE diffusion models for generation steering — exactly our Idea 2 applied to audio instead of text. Key findings: (1) semantic concepts are localized in specific attention layers of diffusion models, (2) SAEs can decompose those activations into interpretable features, (3) steering those features controls the generated output. The architecture is: identify key layers → train SAEs on those layers → steer via SAE features. For text diffusion, we'd do the same: train SAEs on attention layers of a discrete diffusion LM, then steer generation by activating desired SAE features. Also relevant to Idea 1 (SAE features as structured latent space within diffusion) and Idea 4 (SAE-guided denoising at each step).
Open question:
TADA! works on continuous audio diffusion. Does the same localization of semantic concepts hold for discrete text diffusion? DLM-Scope suggests yes — SAEs work even better on diffusion LMs.

children.title: [Feb 13] CorrSteer: Correlation-Based SAE Feature Selection (Aug 2025)

children: Paper:
Cho et al., CorrSteer (arXiv:2508.12535)
Key insight:

Selects steering features by correlating sample correctness with SAE activations at inference time. No contrastive datasets needed. Automates the entire steering pipeline. +3.3% MMLU with only 4000 samples, +27.2% HarmBench with 108 samples.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ METHODOLOGY SUPPORT. The correlation-based feature selection approach could be adapted for selecting which features to include in our diffusion latent space. Instead of using ALL SAE features, we'd pick the ones that correlate with generation quality.

children.title: [Feb 13 1pm] 🔥 Concept Steerers: k-SAE for Image Diffusion Control (Jan 2025)

children: Paper:
Kim et al., 'Concept Steerers: Leveraging K-Sparse Autoencoders for Test-Time Controllable Generations' (arXiv:2501.19066)
Key insight:

Uses k-sparse autoencoders to steer IMAGE diffusion models at test time. They train k-SAEs on text embeddings from prompts, identify monosemantic concepts, then steer generation toward or away from concepts (e.g., remove 'nudity', add 'photographic style'). No retraining, no LoRA, no quality loss.
Results:
20% improvement in unsafe concept removal, 5x faster than SOTA. Code available.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT PRIOR ART! This is exactly our Idea 2 applied to image diffusion. They prove SAE-based steering of diffusion models works. The key question: can we adapt this from image (steering text embeddings → CLIP space) to text diffusion (steering over token space)? Their approach works at the embedding level, which is analogous to what we'd do with text embeddings.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] 🔥🔥 Activation Steering for Masked Diffusion Language Models (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Shnaidman, Feiglin, Yaari, Mentel, Levi, Lapid. 'Activation Steering for Masked Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2512.24143, Dec 2025)
Key insight:

Applies activation steering DIRECTLY to LLaDA-8B-Instruct — a masked diffusion language model. Computes layer-wise steering vectors from a single forward pass using contrastive examples, without simulating the denoising trajectory. These steering directions are applied at EVERY reverse-diffusion step, yielding efficient inference-time control of high-level attributes.
Why this is huge:

This paper proves that activation steering transfers from AR LLMs to masked diffusion LMs. The steering vectors work across transformer sub-modules and can target prompt vs. response tokens separately. The fact that contrastive steering vectors computed from a single forward pass work across ALL denoising steps is remarkable — it means the internal representations of diffusion LMs are stable enough for this kind of intervention.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ THIS IS OUR IDEA 2 IMPLEMENTED! Someone has already done activation steering on text diffusion models. Our SAE-based approach would be the natural next step: instead of manually-designed contrastive steering vectors, use SAE features as the steering basis. This paper provides the proof-of-concept that steering diffusion LMs at inference time is viable.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] SAE-SSV: Supervised Steering in Sparse SAE Spaces (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
He et al., 'SAE-SSV: Supervised Steering in Sparse Representation Spaces for Reliable Control of Language Models' (arXiv:2505.16188, NJIT/Rutgers/Cisco)
Key insight:

Uses SAEs to obtain sparse latent representations, then trains linear classifiers to identify a SMALL SUBSPACE of task-relevant dimensions. Learns supervised steering vectors constrained to this subspace. Critical finding: a notably small subspace is sufficient for effective steering in open-ended generation (sentiment, truthfulness, politics).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT METHODOLOGY for our work. The 'small subspace' finding is crucial — we don't need the full 16K-128K SAE feature space for effective diffusion steering. We can identify a compact, task-relevant subset. Their supervised approach outperforms unsupervised methods in open-ended generation, which is exactly our use case. Code available on GitHub.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] DSAS: Dynamic Steering Across LLMs and Diffusion (Apple, Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Suau, Gonzàlez, Rodríguez (Apple). 'Dynamically Scaled Activation Steering' (arXiv:2512.03661, Dec 2025)
Key insight:

A steering-agnostic framework that decouples WHEN to steer from HOW to steer. Computes context-dependent per-token scaling factors that selectively adjust steering strength. Works on BOTH LLMs AND text-to-image diffusion models without modification. Learns to intervene strongly only when undesired behavior is detected.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ CROSS-MODAL FRAMEWORK. Apple proves that activation steering methods generalize from LLMs to T2I diffusion with zero modification. The dynamic per-token scaling is exactly what we'd want for SAE-guided diffusion: stronger SAE feature enforcement where needed, lighter touch where the model is already on track. Combined with SAE-SSV's subspace selection, this gives us a complete per-step, per-token adaptive steering framework.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] ⚠️ Endogenous Resistance to SAE-Based Steering (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Pepper et al., 'Endogenous Resistance to Activation Steering in Language Models' (arXiv:2602.06941, Feb 2026)
Key insight:

Large LLMs (Llama-3.3-70B) can RESIST SAE-based steering mid-generation, self-correcting back to the original task even when steering is active throughout. They identify 26 SAE latents linked to this 'Endogenous Steering Resistance' (ESR). Zero-ablating these reduces resistance by 25%. Smaller models show much less resistance.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ IMPORTANT CAVEAT for our approach. If we steer diffusion LMs via SAE features, larger models may develop internal resistance to misaligned steering. This is good for safety (models resist adversarial feature injection) but could limit our steering effectiveness. The diffusion setting may be different though — at each denoising step the model starts from noisy input, so it may have less 'context' to detect steering than in AR generation.

children.title: [Feb 17] CB-SAE: Concept Bottleneck SAEs for Better Interpretability + Steerability (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Kulkarni et al., 'Interpretable and Steerable Concept Bottleneck Sparse Autoencoders' (arXiv:2512.10805, Dec 11, 2025)
Key finding:

Systematic analysis reveals a critical limitation: a MAJORITY of SAE neurons exhibit either low interpretability OR low steerability (or both), rendering them ineffective for downstream use. Additionally, user-desired concepts are often absent in unsupervised SAE dictionaries.
Solution:

CB-SAE prunes low-utility neurons and augments the latent space with a lightweight concept bottleneck aligned to user-defined concepts. Results: +32.1% interpretability, +14.5% steerability across LVLMs and image generation.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ ADDRESSES A PRACTICAL LIMITATION. For Idea 2 (feature steering), not all SAE features are equally useful — CB-SAE's pruning + concept alignment could be essential for selecting which features to use for conditional generation. The concept bottleneck approach also bridges to Idea 4: we could use target concepts as the 'dictionary' to guide denoising toward user-specified semantics.

children.title: [Feb 14 11am] 🔥 ILRR: Learning-Free Activation Alignment for Diffusion Steering (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Avrahami & Nachmani, 'ILRR: Inference-Time Steering Method for Masked Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2601.21647, Jan 29, 2026)
Key insight:

Introduces Iterative Latent Representation Refinement — a learning-free framework for steering DLMs by dynamically aligning the internal activations of the generated sequence with those of a reference sequence throughout denoising. No training, no reward model — just activation alignment at each step. Works on both LLaDA and MDLM.
Key results:

10-60% improvement in attribute accuracy over comparable baselines. Introduces 'Spatially Modulated Steering' that enables steering long texts using shorter references by regulating guidance intensity across the sequence. Only one additional forward pass per denoising step.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT BRIDGE TO OUR WORK. ILRR aligns generated activations with reference activations at each denoising step — our Idea 4 (dictionary-guided denoising) would replace 'reference activations' with 'target SAE feature activations'. The framework is already built for DLMs, learning-free, and demonstrates that per-step activation guidance works. Their spatially modulated steering (varying guidance intensity across positions) maps directly to our per-feature semantic weighting concept. This is essentially our Idea 4 minus the SAE component.

children.title: [Feb 13 7pm] 🧪 Our Experiment: Delta-Guided Attention Validates SAE Features as Attractors

children: Source:
Our own experimental work (Feb 13, 2025) — delta-guided attention experiment on SAE feature activations across transformer layers.
Results:

Overall correlation r=0.80, with PEAK correlation r=0.91 at layer 2. This demonstrates that SAE features function as meaningful attractors in the model's residual stream — when you guide attention using deltas between SAE feature activations, the model's behavior is highly predictable.
Why this matters for diffusion+SAE:

This is direct experimental evidence supporting Ideas 1 and 2. If SAE features are meaningful attractors (r=0.80-0.91), then: (1) diffusing in SAE feature space should produce coherent trajectories because the features are real structure, not noise; (2) steering generation toward specific SAE feature activations should reliably shift model output because the features have genuine causal influence. The layer 2 peak (r=0.91) is especially interesting — it suggests early layers may be where SAE features have the strongest attractor dynamics, potentially informing noise schedule design (Idea 3).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ OUR OWN VALIDATION. This bridges our theoretical ideas with experimental evidence. Combined with RouteSAE's multi-layer feature distribution findings, we now have both external and internal evidence that SAE features are real, layer-structured, and causally meaningful — the three properties needed for SAE-guided diffusion to work.

children.title: [Feb 15] 🔥 Steering Diffusion Transformers with SAEs: Multi-Layer Method (Oct 2025)

children: Paper:
First systematic study of SAE steering on Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) — Flux and SD3, not U-Nets.
Key insight:
Multi-layer steering (injecting SAE features across multiple transformer layers simultaneously) dramatically improves causal effectiveness and reduces artifacts compared to single-layer injection.
Feature detection:

Simple similarity-based criterion identifies which layers a concept 'lives in' — guiding where to steer. SAEs trained on both block updates and residual streams reveal which DiT components have strongest causal effects.
Relevance:

Directly validates Idea 2 for image diffusion. The multi-layer finding is crucial — suggests that for text diffusion LMs, steering should also target multiple layers. The layer-selection criterion could transfer to identifying which layers' SAE features are most effective for conditional text generation.
Source:
OpenReview submission 22251, 'Steering Diffusion Transformers with Sparse Autoencoders'

children.title: [Feb 16] CC-Delta: SAE Sparse Space Outperforms Dense Space for Steering (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Assogba et al., 'Sparse Autoencoders are Capable LLM Jailbreak Mitigators' (arXiv:2602.12418, Feb 12, 2026)
Key insight:

Introduces Context-Conditioned Delta Steering (CC-Delta) — identifies SAE features whose activations change when a harmful request is embedded in jailbreak context, then applies inference-time mean-shift steering in SAE latent space. CRITICAL FINDING: steering in sparse SAE feature space CLEARLY OUTPERFORMS dense mean-shift steering on all four models tested, especially against out-of-distribution attacks.
Why this matters for us:

The finding that SAE sparse space outperforms dense activation space for steering is strong evidence for our approach. If SAE feature space provides better steering than raw activations for safety, the same advantage likely holds for generation control. Off-the-shelf SAEs trained for interpretability can be repurposed for practical steering without task-specific training.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ ADDITIONAL VALIDATION for Idea 2. The 'sparse > dense for steering' result strengthens the case for SAE-guided diffusion over raw activation-guided diffusion. The CC-Delta feature selection method (statistical testing on activation deltas) could be adapted for selecting which SAE features to use for generation control.

children.title: 📡 Feb 18, 2026: Gradient-Free Steering via Transferable Concept Vectors (arXiv:2602.11395)

children: STRONG confirmation of Idea 2.

This paper steers unconditional diffusion WITHOUT gradient guidance. Two key observations: (1) even in early corrupted stages, coarse semantic steering works with lightweight offline guidance ('Noise Alignment'), (2) a concept direction learned at one noise level
transfers across both timesteps and samples
. Uses Recursive Feature Machines (RFM), entirely backprop-free.

Their 'transferable concept vectors' are essentially what SAE feature directions would provide. The transferability across timesteps is also encouraging for Idea 3 (noise schedules) — if concept directions persist across noise levels, semantically-aware scheduling becomes more feasible.

children.title: 📡 Feb 18, 2026: SAEs Applied to Diffusion Model Internals (arXiv:2504.15473)

children: DIRECT confirmation
— Cywiński et al. apply SAEs to text-to-image diffusion and find human-interpretable concepts. Scene composition is predictable from SAE features
before the first reverse step completes
. They demonstrate intervention techniques for manipulating image composition and style.

This is the closest existing work to our proposals — but for images, not text. The gap: nobody has done SAE-based steering for TEXT diffusion yet. With discrete diffusion scaling up and SAE steering proven for image diffusion, the text version is the clear next step.

children.title: CASL: Supervised Concept-Aligned SAE for Diffusion (Jan 2026)

children: CASL
(He et al., Jan 21 2026, arXiv:2601.15441) introduces
supervised alignment

between SAE sparse latent dimensions and human-understandable semantic concepts in diffusion models. Trains SAE on frozen U-Net activations, then learns a lightweight linear mapping from concepts to sparse latent dimensions. Validates with CASL-Steer: a controlled latent intervention that shifts activations along concept axes.
Relevance to Idea 2:

This is the CLOSEST existing work to our feature steering proposal, but for images not text. Key advance: they go BEYOND unsupervised SAE features to supervised concept alignment -- meaning each latent dimension reliably corresponds to a known concept. Their Editing Precision Ratio (EPR) metric jointly measures concept specificity and attribute preservation -- exactly what we need for text steering quality evaluation.
Gap:
CASL works on image diffusion (U-Net); nobody has done this for discrete text diffusion. Our contribution would be adapting supervised concept-SAE alignment to text diffusion latent spaces.

children.title: [Feb 19] Steering Diffusion Transformers with SAEs — First Systematic DiT Study (OpenReview, ICLR 2026 submission)

children: Paper:
'Steering Diffusion Transformers with Sparse Autoencoders' (OpenReview #22251, submitted to ICLR 2026)
Key insight:

First systematic study of SAE-based steering for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) — Flux and Stable Diffusion 3. Three contributions: (1) multi-layer steering method that increases causal effectiveness while reducing artifacts, (2) similarity-based criterion for detecting feature presence in residual stream to guide layer selection, (3) SAEs trained on both transformer block updates AND residual stream states to identify which DiT components have strongest causal effects.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT METHODOLOGY for Ideas 2 & 4. The multi-layer steering approach (vs single-layer) and the similarity-based layer selection are directly transferable to text diffusion. Prior image-domain SAE steering work (Concept Steerers, CASL, DiffLens) focused on U-Net architectures; this is the first for transformer-based diffusion — architecturally closer to text DLMs like LLaDA/Dream which also use transformers. The finding that block updates vs residual stream states have different causal profiles informs where to apply SAE-guided denoising.

children.title: Idea 3: Semantically-Aware Noise Schedules

children: Core hypothesis:

Design noise schedules that destroy high-level semantic features last and low-level syntactic features first. During generation (reverse process), coarse semantics (topic, sentiment) emerge first, then fine details (word choice, syntax) fill in — mirroring human writing.

children.title: [Feb 14] On the Role of Discreteness: Core Limitations Identified (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Jin et al., 'On the Role of Discreteness in Diffusion LLMs' (arXiv:2512.22630, Dec 27, 2025)
Key insight:

Identifies TWO core structural limitations in current diffusion LMs: (1) Uniform corruption does not respect how information is distributed across positions — treating all tokens equally ignores positional importance. (2) Token-wise marginal training cannot capture multi-token dependencies during parallel decoding.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ VALIDATES OUR CORE INTUITION for Idea 3! The 'uniform corruption ignores information distribution' critique is exactly why we propose SEMANTIC-aware noise schedules. If different features encode different amounts of information (high-level semantics vs. low-level syntax), uniform noising is suboptimal. SAE features provide a natural basis for NON-uniform corruption based on semantic importance.
New research question:
Can SAE feature importance scores define a corruption schedule that addresses their critique? Features with higher causal influence on generation should be noised last.

children.title: [Feb 13] Evidence from SAE Layer Analysis

children: Source:
Multiple papers (Arad et al. 2025, Cho et al. 2026) confirm the layer-wise pattern:
Early layers → syntactic/input features (word patterns, local context)
Later layers → semantic/output features (topic, style, meaning)
Relevance:

⭐⭐ INDIRECT SUPPORT. The natural layer hierarchy in transformers aligns with our proposed noise schedule. If SAE features from different layers capture different semantic levels, we could design noise that corrupts early-layer features first (add syntactic noise) while preserving late-layer features (keep semantic structure). The reverse process would then naturally reconstruct semantics-first, syntax-last.

children.title: [Feb 13] CARD: Causal Autoregressive Diffusion (2025)

children: Paper:
Li et al., 'Causal Autoregressive Diffusion Language Model' (arXiv:2601.22031)
Key insight:

Unifies causal (left-to-right) structure with diffusion via a 'soft-tailed masking schema' to preserve local context and a 'context-aware reweighting mechanism' derived from signal-to-noise principles. Outperforms MDLM and BD3LM while being 3x more training-efficient.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ NOISE SCHEDULE INSIGHT. Their 'context-aware reweighting' based on signal-to-noise ratio is exactly the kind of adaptive noise scheduling we envision, just applied to positional context rather than semantic level. Could we combine their SNR-based approach with semantic feature levels?

children.title: [Feb 13 1pm] Theory-Informed CFG: Guidance Timing Matters (July 2025)

children: Paper:
Rojas et al., 'Theory-Informed Improvements to Classifier-Free Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2507.08965)
Key insight:

Theoretical analysis of CFG in masked discrete diffusion reveals: HIGH GUIDANCE EARLY IN SAMPLING (when inputs are heavily masked) HARMS quality, while LATE-STAGE guidance has larger effect. Also identifies how current CFG can cause 'imbalanced transitions' like unmasking too rapidly early on.
Solution:
A 'smoothened transport' mechanism achievable via one-line code change. Tested on ImageNet (masked diffusion) and QM9 (uniform diffusion).
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT VALIDATION OF IDEA 3! This is theoretical proof that WHEN you apply guidance during diffusion matters enormously. For semantic noise schedules, this suggests: apply coarse semantic steering late (when it matters most), syntactic/fine-grained steering can be earlier. The 'semantic features last' intuition is validated — late-stage guidance (when structure is mostly formed) is where steering has the most impact.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] 🔥 SAEs Reveal Concept Emergence Timeline in Diffusion (NeurIPS 2025)

children: Paper:
Tinaz et al., 'Emergence and Evolution of Interpretable Concepts in Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2504.15473, NeurIPS 2025)
Key insight:

Applies SAEs to probe a text-to-image diffusion model's activations. Discovers human-interpretable concepts and tracks how they emerge during generation. CRITICAL FINDING: even before the first reverse diffusion step completes, scene composition can be predicted from spatial distribution of activated SAE concepts.
Temporal hierarchy discovered:

(1) Early stages: image COMPOSITION can be effectively controlled via SAE feature intervention. (2) Middle stages: composition finalizes, but STYLE interventions are still effective. (3) Final stages: only minor TEXTURAL details subject to change. This is a coarse→fine semantic hierarchy emerging naturally!
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ STRONGEST VALIDATION OF IDEA 3 YET! This is empirical proof that diffusion models naturally develop a semantic hierarchy across denoising steps — exactly what we want to exploit with semantic noise schedules. Composition → Style → Texture maps directly to our proposed coarse-semantics-first, fine-details-last scheme. AND they use SAEs to discover and control this hierarchy! The key question: does this composition→style→texture hierarchy also hold in TEXT diffusion (topic → tone → word choice)?

children.title: [Feb 14 11am] Where-to-Unmask: Oracle Ordering via Ground-Truth Margins (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Asano et al., 'Where-to-Unmask: Ground-Truth-Guided Unmasking Order Learning for Masked Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2602.09501, Feb 10, 2026)
Key insight:

Introduces Gt-Margin — a position-wise score based on the probability margin between the correct token and its strongest alternative. This yields an oracle unmasking order that prioritizes 'easier' positions first. A supervised planner trained via learning-to-rank imitates this oracle ordering from masked contexts, improving reasoning accuracy without modifying the token prediction model.
Why this matters:

Decouples WHERE-to-unmask from WHAT-to-unmask. The unmasking planner is a separate, lightweight module that plugs into standard MDLM sampling. Shows that heuristic confidence-based orderings (like Margin score) can yield incorrect outputs — learned orderings are better.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ COMPLEMENTS LoMDM for Idea 3. While LoMDM jointly learns ordering + prediction, Where-to-Unmask trains a separate ordering planner. For our SAE-aware noise schedule, this modular approach is very attractive: we could train an SAE-feature-aware unmasking planner that decides generation order based on which SAE features are active/confident, without modifying the base diffusion model. The 'easy positions first' principle maps to 'high-confidence SAE features first'.

children.title: [Feb 14 11am] MDLM Parallelism & Generation Order: Adaptive Behavior at Scale (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Zhong et al., 'Parallelism and Generation Order in Masked Diffusion Language Models: Limits Today, Potential Tomorrow' (arXiv:2601.15593, Jan 22, 2026)
Key insight:

Comprehensive evaluation of 8 MDLMs (up to 100B parameters) on 58 benchmarks. MDLMs exhibit ADAPTIVE decoding behavior: parallelism and generation order vary significantly with task domain, reasoning stage, and whether output is correct. On tasks requiring 'backward information' (e.g., Sudoku), MDLMs naturally adopt solution orders that fill easier positions first.
Generate-then-Edit paradigm:
Proposes theoretical motivation for a two-phase approach: parallel generation followed by editing, which mitigates inter-token dependency loss while retaining parallel decoding efficiency.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ EMPIRICAL GROUNDING for Idea 3. The finding that MDLMs already adapt their generation order per-task validates our intuition that non-uniform ordering matters. The Generate-then-Edit paradigm could work with our approach: generate with SAE-guided semantic ordering, then edit with SAE feature constraints. The 'dependency loss from parallelism' finding is an important caveat — our semantic noise schedule should preserve key inter-token dependencies.

children.title: [Feb 16] 🔥🔥🔥 HDLM: Hierarchical Diffusion Language Models — Our Idea 3 Formalized! (NeurIPS 2025)

children: Paper:
Zhou et al., 'Next Semantic Scale Prediction via Hierarchical Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2510.08632, NeurIPS 2025)
Key insight:

Introduces a HIERARCHICAL VOCABULARY where low-level tokens (detailed semantics) are surjectively mapped to high-level tokens (coarse-grained meanings). In the forward process, each token is independently perturbed to its higher-level semantic ancestor according to a scheduler. In the reverse process, the model progressively predicts the next, more detailed semantic scale. This is literally 'next semantic scale prediction' — generating from coarse meaning to fine-grained tokens.
Technical details:

They derive closed-form ELBO expressions and show HDLM generalizes MDLM (which is a special case). Achieves consistently lower validation AND generative perplexity than baselines. The hierarchical vocabulary provides a principled way to define what 'semantic levels' mean — not hand-wavy, but grounded in vocabulary structure.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ THIS IS OUR IDEA 3 IMPLEMENTED! The key difference: HDLM uses vocabulary hierarchy (e.g., WordNet hypernyms or BPE tree) to define semantic levels, while we propose using SAE feature importance/abstraction level. The two approaches are complementary: HDLM's vocabulary hierarchy operates at the token level, while SAE features capture representation-level semantics (topic, style, sentiment). A combined approach — HDLM's coarse→fine token generation PLUS SAE feature-guided noise schedules for representation-level semantics — could be very powerful.
New research question:

Can SAE features define a LEARNED semantic hierarchy that's richer than vocabulary-based hierarchy? HDLM's hierarchy is fixed by vocabulary structure; SAE features could provide a data-driven, context-dependent hierarchy.

children.title: [Feb 17] Tuning the Implicit Regularizer: Optimized Mask Schedules for MDLMs (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Huang & Mirzasoleiman, 'Tuning the Implicit Regularizer of Masked Diffusion Language Models: Enhancing Generalization via Insights from k-Parity' (arXiv:2601.22450, Jan 30, 2026, UCLA)
Key insight:

Theoretically decomposes the Masked Diffusion objective into a SIGNAL regime (drives feature learning) and a NOISE regime (serves as implicit regularizer). Critically, they show that optimizing the DISTRIBUTION of mask probabilities — not just a fixed schedule — significantly improves perplexity: up to 8.8% gain on 8B-parameter models during pre-training and 5.8% during SFT. The MD objective fundamentally alters the learning landscape vs. AR, enabling rapid generalization without grokking.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ THEORETICAL GROUNDING for Idea 3. If optimizing mask probability distribution gives 5-9% gains, then our proposal for SEMANTICALLY-aware mask distributions (noise more for syntactic tokens, preserve semantic tokens) could yield even larger gains by aligning the mask schedule with information content rather than treating all positions equally. Their Signal/Noise regime decomposition provides a theoretical framework: we want SAE-defined semantic features to stay in the Signal regime (learned early, preserved) while low-level details are in the Noise regime (regularized, filled in last).

children.title: [Feb 17] CoM-DAD: Semantic Manifold + Variable-Rate Noise Schedule (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Xu et al., 'Bridging the Discrete-Continuous Gap: Unified Multimodal Generation via Coupled Manifold Discrete Absorbing Diffusion' (arXiv:2601.04056, Jan 7, 2026)
Key insight:

Decouples high-level semantic planning from low-level token synthesis via a DUAL-PROCESS architecture: (1) continuous latent diffusion models the semantic manifold, (2) discrete absorbing diffusion generates tokens with a VARIABLE-RATE NOISE SCHEDULE conditioned on evolving semantic priors. The semantic diffusion provides context that guides the token-level noise schedule.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ COMBINES IDEAS 1 & 3! CoM-DAD implements BOTH a structured continuous latent space (semantic manifold, like our Idea 1) AND a semantically-conditioned noise schedule (like our Idea 3). The variable-rate noise schedule is exactly what we proposed: noise rates that depend on semantic content rather than being uniform. For SAE integration: replace their 'semantic manifold' with SAE feature space → interpretable semantics guide variable token-level noise rates.
Key advantage:
Their Stochastic Mixed-Modal Transport aligns modalities without heavy contrastive encoders. This could simplify SAE integration — no need for separate alignment training.

props.title: Feb 20, 2026: Noise Type Affects Scaling — Semantic Schedules Have Real Bite

children.children: The scaling law study (2602.15014) shows noise type profoundly affects model behavior — masked, uniform, and interpolating diffusion have fundamentally different scaling curves. This validates our Idea 3 hypothesis: if even the basic noise distribution matters this much, a semantically-aware schedule (destroying high-level features last) should have even larger effects on generation quality.

children.title: [Feb 23] NeoDiff: Non-Simultaneous Continuous Diffusion with Per-Token Semantic Noise (ACL 2025)

children: Paper:
'Unifying Continuous and Discrete Text Diffusion with Non-simultaneous Diffusion Processes' (arXiv:2505.22165, ACL 2025)
Key insight:

NeoDiff introduces a Poisson diffusion process where different tokens receive different noise levels, and a time predictor that adaptively modulates denoising progress based on token semantics. Unlike uniform corruption (all tokens noised equally) or discrete masking (binary mask/unmask), NeoDiff provides fine-grained, continuous, per-token noise control conditioned on semantic content.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT IMPLEMENTATION OF IDEA 3! NeoDiff’s per-token semantic noise modulation is essentially a semantic-aware noise schedule. Their ‘time predictor’ learns which tokens need more/less denoising based on content — exactly our hypothesis that high-level semantic features should be destroyed last. The key extension: replace their learned time predictor with SAE feature-based scheduling, where noise levels are determined by which SAE features (low-level syntactic vs high-level semantic) are active at each position. NeoDiff outperforms both discrete and continuous diffusion baselines, validating that non-uniform semantic noise is strictly better than uniform.

children.title: Idea 4: Dictionary-Guided Denoising

children: Core hypothesis:

At each denoising step, project the intermediate representation onto the nearest sparse combination of known SAE features. The SAE dictionary acts as a 'parts list' that constrains the denoising trajectory to pass through interpretable states.

children.title: [Feb 13] Guidance Mechanisms for Discrete Diffusion Models (Dec 2024)

children: Paper:
Schiff et al., 'Simple Guidance Mechanisms for Discrete Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2412.10193), from the Kuleshov group (same team as MDLM)
Key insight:

Implements classifier-free guidance (D-CFG) and classifier-based guidance (D-CBG) for discrete diffusion models (MDLM and their new UDLM). The classifier-based approach trains a classifier on noisy intermediate states and uses gradients to guide denoising.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT INFRASTRUCTURE. Their D-CBG framework is exactly the architecture we'd plug into! Instead of training a task-specific classifier for guidance, we'd use SAE feature detection as the guide. At each denoising step, measure which SAE features are active, compare to target features, and bias the denoising toward the desired feature profile.
They already support MDLM + UDLM + AR baselines with guidance. Code is open-source. This could be our implementation starting point.

children.title: [Feb 13] EDLM: Energy-Based Diffusion Language Model (ICLR 2025)

children: Paper:
Xu et al., 'Energy-Based Diffusion Language Models for Text Generation' (arXiv:2410.21357, ICLR 2025)
Key insight:

Introduces an energy-based model operating at the full sequence level for each diffusion step, in residual form. The EBM corrects the standard diffusion approximation, achieving state-of-the-art among diffusion LMs and approaching AR perplexity. Can leverage pretrained AR models.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ ARCHITECTURE INSIGHT. The residual EBM at each step is philosophically similar to our 'project onto SAE features' idea. Both add a correction/constraint at each denoising step. We could potentially formulate SAE projection as an energy function: lower energy when the representation is close to a sparse SAE reconstruction.

children.title: [Feb 13 1pm] SLCD: Supervised Learning Controllable Diffusion (May 2025)

children: Paper:
Oertell et al., 'Efficient Controllable Diffusion via Optimal Classifier Guidance' (arXiv:2505.21666)
Key insight:

Frames controllable generation as KL-regularized optimization. Iteratively generates online data, trains a SMALL CLASSIFIER to guide diffusion. The classifier is trained on-the-fly to guide toward desired objectives. Provably converges to optimal solution.
Results:
Works on both continuous (image) AND discrete (biological sequence) diffusion. Same inference time as base model. Code available.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ METHODOLOGY FOR IDEA 4! Instead of a task-specific classifier, we could use SAE feature detection as the 'classifier'. The SLCD framework shows how to efficiently train a small model to guide diffusion toward objectives — our 'objective' would be matching target SAE feature activations. Their theoretical framework (KL-regularized + no-regret learning) could provide guarantees for SAE-guided denoising.

children.title: [Feb 13 3pm] DiffLens: Mechanistic Interpretability for Diffusion Control (CVPR 2025)

children: Paper:
Li et al., 'Dissecting and Mitigating Diffusion Bias via Mechanistic Interpretability' (arXiv:2503.20483, CVPR 2025)
Key insight:

Identifies specific 'bias features' — internal decision-making mechanisms — within diffusion model architectures via mechanistic interpretability. By directly manipulating these features, they achieve granular control over generation attributes (gender, race in images) while preserving output quality. Works on both unconditional AND conditional diffusion models.
Critical detail:

They discover that different intrinsic features control different fine-grained aspects of generation — some control composition, others control attributes. This internal factorization is exactly the kind of 'parts list' our dictionary-guided denoising idea proposes.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT SUPPORT for Idea 4. This proves that diffusion models have identifiable, manipulable internal features that map to interpretable generation attributes. Our SAE dictionary would serve the same role as their 'bias features' but in a more systematic, complete way — the SAE dictionary gives us ALL the features, not just bias-related ones. Their manipulation technique (direct feature intervention at each step) is exactly our dictionary-guided denoising.

children.title: [Feb 14 11am] EntRGi: Entropy-Aware Reward Guidance for Discrete Diffusion (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Tejaswi et al., 'EntRGi: Entropy Aware Reward Guidance for Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2602.05000, Feb 4, 2026)
Key insight:

Addresses the fundamental challenge of reward guidance in DISCRETE diffusion: you can't differentiate through discrete tokens. Existing approaches either use continuous relaxations (degrading gradient quality since reward models were never trained on continuous inputs) or straight-through estimators (incorrect optimization). EntRGi dynamically modulates the continuous relaxation using the model's confidence/entropy, getting the best of both worlds.
Results:
Tested on 7B-parameter diffusion LM across 3 reward models and 3 multi-skill benchmarks. Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art reward guidance methods.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ SOLVES A KEY TECHNICAL CHALLENGE for Idea 4. If we want to guide discrete diffusion toward target SAE features, we face the same discrete/continuous gradient mismatch. EntRGi's entropy-aware relaxation provides a principled way to compute guidance gradients: when the model is confident (low entropy), use discrete tokens for accurate reward/feature signals; when uncertain (high entropy), use continuous relaxation for smooth gradients. This is exactly the adaptive guidance mechanism our dictionary-guided denoising needs.

props.title: [Feb 15] Residual Context Diffusion (RCD): Recycling Discarded Computation (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Hu et al., 'Residual Context Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2601.22954, Jan 30, 2026, UC Berkeley/Apple)
Key insight:

In block-wise dLLMs using remasking, tokens that don't pass the confidence threshold still carry useful contextual information. RCD converts these discarded representations into 'contextual residuals' and injects them into the next denoising step. Result: 5-10 point accuracy gains, nearly doubles AIME accuracy, 4-5x fewer denoising steps.
Connection to Idea 4:

⭐⭐ The 'residual' concept directly parallels SAE residuals. In SAE-guided denoising, projecting onto sparse SAE features inherently creates a residual (the part of the activation not captured by any dictionary feature). RCD shows that these residuals contain valuable contextual information that improves generation when recycled. Implication: our dictionary-guided denoising should NOT discard the SAE residual — it should be passed forward as context, exactly as RCD does with discarded token representations.

props.title: [Feb 15] Corrective Diffusion Language Models: Teaching DLMs to Self-Correct (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:
Zhang et al., 'Corrective Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2512.15596, Dec 2025/Jan 2026)
Key insight:

Standard MDLM training only teaches unmasking — models cannot distinguish correct from incorrect visible tokens and thus cannot reliably refine. CDLM post-training explicitly supervises visible incorrect tokens, enabling discriminative confidence and targeted correction.
Connection to Idea 4:

⭐⭐ This addresses a fundamental issue for dictionary-guided denoising: if the model can't tell which tokens need refinement, per-step SAE feature guidance won't help — the model won't know where to apply it. CDLM's corrective training could serve as a prerequisite or co-training objective: first teach the model to identify errors (CDLM), then use SAE features to guide the corrections (our Idea 4). The finding that uniform corruption ignores positional information distribution also aligns with our semantic noise schedule hypothesis (Idea 3).

children.title: 📡 Feb 18, 2026: SAEs Applied to Diffusion Model Internals (arXiv:2504.15473)

children: DIRECT confirmation
— Cywiński et al. apply SAEs to text-to-image diffusion and find human-interpretable concepts. Scene composition is predictable from SAE features
before the first reverse step completes
. They demonstrate intervention techniques for manipulating image composition and style.

This is the closest existing work to our proposals — but for images, not text. The gap: nobody has done SAE-based steering for TEXT diffusion yet. With discrete diffusion scaling up and SAE steering proven for image diffusion, the text version is the clear next step.

children.title: The State of Text Diffusion (Landscape)

children: The field exploded in late 2024 / early 2025. Here are the key models and what they tell us:

children.title: Key Models Timeline

children: SEDD
(Lou et al., ICML 2024 Best Paper 🏆) — Score entropy for discrete diffusion. First principled score-matching in discrete space.
MDLM
(Sahoo et al., NeurIPS 2024) — Simplified masked diffusion = mixture of MLM losses. Outperforms SEDD. The foundation most new work builds on.
LLaDA
(Nie et al., Feb 2025) — 8B-parameter diffusion LM trained from scratch. Rivals LLaMA3 8B. Beats GPT-4o on reversal tasks. Proves diffusion scales.
Mercury
(Inception Labs, Feb 2025) — 1,109 tokens/sec on H100s. ~19x faster than GPT-4o Mini at comparable quality. Commercial model.
DiffuLLaMA
(Gong et al., 2024) — Converted LLaMA2 7B to diffusion. Shows AR→diffusion adaptation works.
CARD
(Li et al., Jan 2025) — Causal diffusion with KV-cache support. 1.7-4x inference speedup. Bridges AR efficiency with diffusion parallelism.
EDLM
(Xu et al., ICLR 2025) — Energy-based correction at each step. SOTA diffusion LM perplexity.
Gemini Diffusion
(Google, May 2025) — Google's entry into text diffusion. 1,479 tokens/sec. State-of-the-art.
Dream 7B
(HKU/Huawei, Aug 2025) — Most powerful open diffusion LM. Matches Qwen2.5-7B on general/math/code. Any-order generation.
LLaDA2.0
(Ant Group, Dec 2025) — First 100B diffusion LM using MoE. Proves frontier-scale text diffusion is viable. Matches Qwen3-30B.
FS-DFM
(Apple, ICLR 2026, arXiv:2509.20624) — Few-Step Discrete Flow-Matching. 8 steps matches 1024-step baseline perplexity. 128x faster sampling. Makes text diffusion practical for real deployment.
RCD

(Hu et al., Jan 2026, arXiv:2601.22954) — Residual Context Diffusion. Recycles computation from discarded tokens as contextual residuals. 5-10 point accuracy gains, nearly doubles AIME accuracy, 4-5x fewer steps.
CDLM

(Zhang et al., Dec 2025/Jan 2026, arXiv:2512.15596) — Corrective Diffusion Language Models. Post-training for corrective behavior: models learn to assign lower confidence to incorrect tokens and iteratively refine them. Key finding: standard MDLM training fails to teach correction.
LLaDA2.1
(Ant Group, Feb 2026, arXiv:2602.08676) — Token-to-Token editing + Mask-to-Token decoding. 892 TPS on HumanEval+. First large-scale RL for dLLMs. Speedy and Quality modes.
DiDi-Instruct
(Zheng et al., Oct 2025, arXiv:2509.25035) — 64x acceleration of dLLMs via KL-divergence distillation. Ppl 18.4 at 128 NFEs.
Scaling Beyond MDLMs

(Sahoo et al., Feb 16, 2026, arXiv:2602.15014) — From the MDLM author! First scaling law study across discrete diffusion families. Uniform-state diffusion outperforms AR and masked diffusion on GSM8K despite worse perplexity. Challenges masked diffusion dominance.
CCDD

(Zhou et al., Oct 2025, arXiv:2510.03206) — Coevolutionary Continuous Discrete Diffusion. Joint diffusion in continuous representation + discrete token space. Continuous latents serve as implicit planning that guides token decoding.
CADD

(Zheng et al., Oct 2025, arXiv:2510.01329) — Continuously Augmented Discrete Diffusion. Augments discrete [MASK] space with paired continuous latent diffusion. Masked tokens get informative latent vectors instead of void. Consistent gains on text, image, and code.
XDLM
(Zhao et al., Feb 2026, arXiv:2602.01362) — Bridges MDLM/UDLM via stationary noise kernel. Unified framework recovering each as special case. Pareto-optimal for understanding + generation.
Mechanism Shift

(Kong et al., Jan 2026, arXiv:2601.14758) — Post-training from AR to MDM reorganizes computation: distributed integration replaces localized specialization, increased early-layer processing for global planning.
Tuning MDL Regularizer
(Huang & Mirzasoleiman, Jan 2026, arXiv:2601.22450) — Optimized mask probability distribution yields 5.8-8.8% perplexity gains on 8B models. Signal/Noise decomposition of MD objective.
CoM-DAD

(Xu et al., Jan 2026, arXiv:2601.04056) — Coupled Manifold Discrete Absorbing Diffusion. Dual-process: continuous semantic manifold + discrete tokens with variable-rate noise schedule conditioned on semantics.
CB-SAE
(Kulkarni et al., Dec 2025, arXiv:2512.10805) — Concept Bottleneck SAEs. Prunes low-utility neurons, adds concept-aligned bottleneck. +32.1% interpretability, +14.5% steerability.
FLM/FMLM

(Kim et al., Feb 18, 2026, arXiv:2602.16813) — Flow-based Language Model via continuous denoising over one-hot encodings. One-step generation exceeds 8-step discrete diffusion quality. Challenges discrete diffusion assumption.
DSL
(Wu et al., Feb 18, 2026, arXiv:2602.16169) — Discrete Stochastic Localization. SNR-invariant denoiser across corruption continuum. 4x fewer steps than MDLM+ReMDM. Enables self-correction.
P2
(ICLR 2026, OpenReview #11824) — Path Planning for MDM sampling. Decomposes steps into planning + denoising. Enables token refinement. +33% code pass@1, +68% story ROUGE.
Sampler Correctness
(Tang et al., Feb 23 2026, arXiv:2602.19619) — Oracle HMM evaluation proves few-step discrete diffusion samplers are distributionally incorrect. LLaDA shows entropy collapse.
Reward-Guided Stitching
(Miles et al., Feb 26 2026, arXiv:2602.22871) — Step-level recombination of DLM reasoning traces via PRM scoring. Training-free, +23.8% accuracy, 1.8x faster.
Key takeaway:

Text diffusion is no longer a niche curiosity. It's scaling to frontier-model quality. The infrastructure for our SAE experiments is ready — MDLM and its variants provide solid open-source foundations.

children.title: [Feb 15] Test-Time Scaling for Block Diffusion LMs (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
'Advancing Block Diffusion Language Models for Test-Time Scaling' — unified framework for test-time compute scaling in BDLMs.
Key innovations:

BACD (Bounded Adaptive Confidence Decoding) dynamically adjusts denoising steps based on model confidence. 'Think Coarse, Critic Fine' (TCCF) uses large blocks for exploration, small blocks for refinement. Progressive Block Size Extension mitigates quality loss when scaling up block size.
Results:
2.26x speedup and +11.2 points on reasoning benchmarks over TraDo-8B. Shows diffusion LMs can benefit from test-time compute scaling like AR models.
Relevance:

The adaptive confidence-based denoising connects to Idea 3 (semantically-aware schedules) — the model learns where it needs more refinement. TCCF's coarse→fine paradigm mirrors our intuition that high-level features should be resolved first.
Source:

children.title: [Feb 16] LLaDA2.1: Token Editing + First Large-Scale RL for dLLMs (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Bie et al. (50 authors), 'LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing' (arXiv:2602.08676, Feb 9, 2026)
Key innovations:

(1) Joint Token-to-Token (T2T) editing + Mask-to-Token (M2T) decoding scheme. Speedy Mode aggressively lowers M2T threshold then refines via T2T; Quality Mode uses conservative thresholds. (2) First large-scale RL framework for dLLMs with specialized stable gradient estimation. (3) Released LLaDA2.1-Mini (16B) and LLaDA2.1-Flash (100B).
Performance:
892 TPS on HumanEval+, 801 TPS on BigCodeBench, 663 TPS on LiveCodeBench — at 100B scale. Evaluated on 33 benchmarks.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ INFRASTRUCTURE. The T2T editing scheme is interesting for our work — it means dLLMs can now EDIT existing tokens, not just unmask. For SAE-guided denoising (Idea 4), T2T editing could be the mechanism by which SAE feature constraints are enforced: detect tokens that don't match target SAE features, then edit them. The RL framework for dLLMs could also be used to train SAE-feature-aware generation policies.

children.title: [Feb 16] Prism: Hierarchical Search + Self-Verification for dLLM Scaling (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Bai et al., 'Prism: Efficient Test-Time Scaling via Hierarchical Search and Self-Verification for Discrete Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2602.01842, Feb 2, 2026)
Key insight:

Hierarchical Trajectory Search (HTS) dynamically prunes and reallocates compute in early-to-mid denoising. Local branching with partial remasking explores diverse outputs while preserving high-confidence tokens. Self-Verified Feedback (SVF) replaces external verifiers. Tested on LLaDA 8B, Dream 7B, LLaDA 2.0-mini.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ The hierarchical trajectory search is relevant to Idea 3 — it dynamically allocates compute based on what stage of denoising needs it most. The partial remasking could be combined with SAE feature analysis: remask tokens whose SAE features don't match the target profile.

children.title: [Feb 17] 🔥 Scaling Beyond Masked Diffusion: Perplexity Is Misleading Across Families (Feb 16, 2026)

children: Paper:
Sahoo et al. (MDLM author!), 'Scaling Beyond Masked Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2602.15014, Feb 16, 2026)
Key insight:

First scaling law study across discrete diffusion families (masked, uniform-state, interpolating). CRITICAL FINDING: perplexity is informative WITHIN a family but MISLEADING ACROSS families. Uniform-state diffusion at 1.7B outperforms both AR and masked diffusion on GSM8K despite worse validation perplexity.
