The speed-quality Pareto frontier favors different methods than perplexity alone suggests.
Practical finding:
Masked diffusion can be made ~12% more FLOPs-efficient with a simple cross-entropy objective.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ IMPORTANT FOR ALL IDEAS. If we build SAE-guided diffusion, we should not default to masked diffusion just because it has best perplexity. Uniform-state diffusion may be a better base for SAE integration — it operates on all tokens simultaneously (not just masked ones), which aligns better with SAE features that capture whole-sequence properties. The cross-family comparison methodology is also relevant: we'll need to evaluate SAE-guided diffusion on downstream tasks, not just perplexity.

children.title: [Feb 17] CCDD: Joint Continuous-Discrete Diffusion for Latent Reasoning (Oct 2025)

children: Paper:
Zhou et al., 'Coevolutionary Continuous Discrete Diffusion: Make Your Diffusion Language Model a Latent Reasoner' (arXiv:2510.03206, Oct 2025)
Key insight:

Defines a joint multimodal diffusion process on the union of a continuous representation space and a discrete token space. A single model simultaneously denoises in both spaces. The continuous latents serve as implicit planning/reasoning that guides discrete token decoding — they generate faster and provide high-level guidance.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT ARCHITECTURE for Idea 1. CCDD already implements the two-space paradigm we envision: continuous representation space (where SAE features live) + discrete token space. Our proposal would replace their generic continuous space with SAE feature space, making the continuous latents interpretable. The 'continuous guides discrete' dynamic is exactly what SAE-structured latent spaces should provide: high-level SAE features guide the token-level denoising.
New research question:
Can we plug SAE feature space into CCDD's continuous component? The architecture already supports joint continuous-discrete denoising — we'd just make the continuous space interpretable via SAEs.

children.title: [Feb 17] SearchDiff: Training-Free Constrained Generation for Masked Diffusion (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Ta et al., 'Search-Augmented Masked Diffusion Models for Constrained Generation' (arXiv:2602.02727, Feb 2026)
Key insight:

At each denoising step, model predictions define a proposal set that is optimized under user-specified property constraints, yielding modified reverse transitions that steer sampling toward feasible solutions. Training-free — works with any masked diffusion model.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT FRAMEWORK for Idea 4. SearchDiff's 'property satisfaction function' at each denoising step is exactly where SAE feature detection would plug in. Instead of generic constraints, use SAE feature activations as the optimization target — steer each denoising step toward sparse combinations of desired dictionary features. No retraining needed.

children.title: [Feb 17] Mechanistic Indicators of SAE Steering Effectiveness (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Jafari et al., 'Mechanistic Indicators of Steering Effectiveness in Large Language Models' (arXiv:2602.01716, Feb 2026)
Key insight:

Introduces entropy-based Normalized Branching Factor (NBF) and KL divergence as mechanistic predictors of when SAE-based and CAA steering will succeed or fail. Provides the first diagnostic framework for steering reliability.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ Methodology for Idea 2. In SAE-guided diffusion, we'd need to know WHEN during the denoising process steering is most effective. NBF and KL diagnostics could predict which denoising steps benefit most from SAE feature intervention, complementing the 'late-stage guidance is better' finding from Theory-Informed CFG.

children.title: [Feb 17] Falsifying SAE Reasoning Features — Important Caveat (Jan 2026)

children: Paper:
Ma et al., 'Falsifying Sparse Autoencoder Reasoning Features in Language Models' (arXiv:2601.05679, Jan 2026)
Key insight:

45-90% of contrastively selected 'reasoning' SAE features activate from just a few associated token injections into non-reasoning text. Many SAE features capture low-dimensional cues co-occurring with reasoning, not reasoning itself. Steering with these features yields minimal benchmark changes.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ Important caveat for all ideas. For SAE-guided diffusion, we must ensure features used for steering capture genuine semantic properties, not surface cues. This reinforces the input/output feature distinction from Arad et al. — 'output features' that causally change model behavior are more reliable than 'input features' that merely detect patterns. Falsification testing should be part of our feature selection pipeline.

children.title: [Feb 17] T3D — Few-Step Diffusion Language Models (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Zhang et al., 'T3D: Few-Step Diffusion Language Models via Trajectory Self-Distillation with Direct Discriminative Optimization' (arXiv:2602.12262, Feb 2026)
Key insight:

DLLMs need many refinement steps, making them slow in practice. T3D uses trajectory self-distillation — distilling the model's own multi-step trajectories into fewer steps — combined with Direct Discriminative Optimization (DDO), a reverse-KL objective that encourages the student to concentrate on high-probability teacher modes. Built on the SDAR (semi-autoregressive diffusion) model family. Substantially narrows the gap between few-step and full-step decoding.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ Practical efficiency for Idea 1/2. If SAE-guided diffusion adds overhead per denoising step, reducing total steps via distillation becomes critical. T3D's approach could be applied after SAE-guided training to produce a fast inference model. The DDO objective (mode-seeking rather than mode-covering) is also interesting — it may interact well with SAE feature steering by keeping generation focused on interpretable modes.

children.title: [Feb 17] LLaDA2.1 — Token Editing for Fast Text Diffusion (Feb 2026)

children: Paper:
Bie et al., 'LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing' (arXiv:2602.08676, Feb 2026)
Key insight:

LLaDA2.1 introduces token editing as a paradigm for accelerating masked diffusion language models. Rather than re-predicting all masked tokens each step, selectively edit tokens that need refinement. This addresses the speed-quality tradeoff that has limited practical deployment of DLLMs.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ Direct descendant of LLaDA (our key reference model). Token editing is highly complementary to SAE-guided diffusion: SAE features could identify WHICH tokens need editing (those with low-confidence or misaligned features), creating a natural synergy. Instead of uniform denoising, SAE analysis could prioritize tokens where semantic features are most uncertain. This is the LLaDA ecosystem maturing — exactly the foundation we'd build on.

children.title: Open Questions

children: Invertibility:
Is the SAE encoder → decoder mapping good enough that diffusing in SAE space and decoding back produces coherent text? SAEs are trained for reconstruction, but small errors compound.
Analysis vs. Generation features:
SAEs are trained to analyze existing text activations. Are those same features useful for generation? The input/output feature distinction (Arad et al.) suggests we need output features specifically.
Which diffusion framework?
MDLM (discrete, mask-based) vs. continuous latent diffusion vs. CARD (causal). For SAE integration, continuous latent diffusion might be most natural since SAE activations are continuous.
Sparsity during diffusion:
SAE activations are sparse. During the noisy intermediate steps of diffusion, will sparsity be maintained or destroyed? If destroyed, the interpretability benefit disappears.
Computational cost:
Running SAE encode/decode at each diffusion step could be expensive. EDLM's residual approach might offer a lighter alternative.
Scale mismatch:
Current SAEs are trained on models up to ~7B. Text diffusion models are also at ~7-8B. But SAE dictionaries have 16K-128K features. Is this the right dimensionality for a diffusion latent space?

children.title: 📡 Feb 25, 2026: Research Sweep — 3 New Findings
[Feb 25] SAeUron: Interpretable Concept Unlearning in Diffusion Models with SAEs (ICML 2025)

children: Cywiński & Deja, 'SAeUron: Interpretable Concept Unlearning in Diffusion Models with Sparse Autoencoders' (arXiv:2501.18052, ICML 2025)

SAeUron trains SAEs on image diffusion model activations and identifies specific SAE features corresponding to concepts (e.g., 'nudity', 'violence'). By zeroing those features, they achieve concept erasure that is robust to adversarial attacks — unlike other methods. A single SAE can remove multiple concepts simultaneously. Code and checkpoints available.

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT VALIDATION of Ideas 2 & 4 in the image domain. SAeUron proves that individual SAE features causally control concept presence in diffusion generation. The inverse of erasure is enhancement — exactly what Feature Steering (Idea 2) proposes. For Dictionary-Guided Denoising (Idea 4), SAeUron's finding that features are robustly causal (surviving adversarial attacks) means SAE-based guidance would be reliable, not just correlational. The multi-concept capability via a single SAE aligns with our vision of a unified SAE dictionary guiding diverse generation properties.
Transfer to text:

SAeUron operates on U-Net activations in image diffusion. For text DLMs (transformer-based), the same approach would use SAEs on transformer hidden states — which CorrSteer and DLM-Scope already do for LLMs/DLMs respectively. The pipeline is: train SAE → identify causal features → steer during generation.

children.title: [Feb 25] CD4LM: Consistency Distillation for Diffusion Language Models (Jan 2026)

children: Liang et al., 'CD4LM: Consistency Distillation and aDaptive Decoding for Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2601.02236, Jan 5, 2026)

CD4LM decouples DLM training from inference via Discrete-Space Consistency Distillation (DSCD) and Confidence-Adaptive Decoding (CAD). Achieves up to 14x faster inference without sacrificing quality. The student learns to map noisy states directly to clean outputs in fewer steps, while CAD adaptively decides which tokens to finalize based on confidence.

⭐⭐ Relevant to Idea 4 (Dictionary-Guided Denoising). Fewer denoising steps means each step carries more weight — SAE guidance per step becomes more impactful. The Confidence-Adaptive Decoding is interesting: tokens are finalized when confidence is high. SAE feature coherence could serve as an additional confidence signal — if SAE features align into a coherent concept, that's a sign the token is ready to be finalized.
Connection to Idea 3:
CAD's per-token adaptive scheduling is a form of semantically-aware scheduling. SAE features could inform which tokens are 'semantically settled' vs still needing refinement.

children.title: [Feb 25] Sparse-LaViDa: Sparse Multimodal Discrete Diffusion at 10.4B Scale (Dec 2025)

children: Li et al., 'Sparse-LaViDa: Sparse Multimodal Discrete Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2512.14008, Dec 16, 2025)

Sparse-LaViDa dynamically truncates unnecessary masked tokens at each inference step to accelerate MDM sampling. Built on LaViDa-O, a 10.4B unified diffusion model supporting image understanding, text-to-image, and image editing. The key insight: not all masked positions need attention at every step — sparse computation focuses on the 'active frontier'.

⭐⭐ Relevant to Idea 3 (Semantically-Aware Noise Schedules). Sparse-LaViDa's dynamic token truncation is an implicit form of adaptive scheduling — tokens that are 'easy' get less compute. SAE features could make this explicit: tokens whose SAE feature activations are already coherent could be deprioritized, while tokens in semantically ambiguous states get more denoising attention. The multimodal setting (text + image) is also relevant: SAE features could bridge modalities in the shared latent space.

children.title: 📡 Feb 26, 2026: Steerling-8B — Our Idea 2, Built at Scale

children.content: 🔴 **Steerling-8B** (Guide Labs) is the first inherently interpretable 8B language model, built on a causal discrete diffusion backbone. It decomposes embeddings into ~33K supervised 'known' concepts + ~100K discovered concepts + residual, and can suppress or amplify specific concepts at inference time WITHOUT retraining. 84%+ of token contribution routes through the concept module. Trained on 1.35T tokens, competitive with models using 2-7× more compute.

children.title: Why This Matters for Our Research

children.content: This is a direct, large-scale validation of our Idea 2 (Feature Steering for Conditional Generation). They built exactly what we theorized: a model where you can activate interpretable features to control generation. Their key innovation is constraining the training loss to force signal through concepts — this is the missing architectural detail we hadn't specified. Also validates Idea 1 (their concept decomposition IS a structured latent space). The discrete diffusion backbone means multi-token steering, not just next-token.
🟡 **IDLM** (2602.19066): Distills diffusion LMs to reduce inference steps by 4-64× while preserving quality. Practical for deploying any SAE+diffusion system.

🟡 **Discrete Diffusion for Lookahead Planning** (2602.19980): Shows discrete diffusion develops emergent mechanisms different from AR models for multi-step planning — relevant to noise schedule design (Idea 3).

children.title: 📡 Feb 26, 2026 (1pm): VDLM, Semantic Bottleneck & Simplified USDM Training

children.content: 🔴 **VDLM: Variable Diffusion LMs via Robust Latent-to-Text Rendering** (arXiv:2602.15870, Jan 27 2026): Separates semantic planning from text rendering — applies LLaDA-style masked diffusion over *semantic variable embeddings* for iterative refinement in latent space, then uses a Vec2Text renderer to decode. Post-trains the planner with embedding-space RL rewards, avoiding text decoding inside the RL loop. Competitive on 9 benchmarks spanning reasoning, math, and code.

children.title: Why VDLM Matters for Our Research

children.content: VDLM is a strong validation of Idea 1 (Structured Latent Spaces). Their architecture explicitly separates diffusion planning in embedding space from text rendering — exactly the modular design we envision. The key insight: you can do RL post-training entirely in embedding space without decoding to text. For our SAE-guided approach, this suggests: (1) diffuse in SAE feature space for planning, (2) use a separate renderer to produce text. Their Vec2Text + embedding perturbations for robustness is relevant to Idea 4 — the renderer must be robust to noisy/approximate latent representations, just as our dictionary-guided denoising would produce approximate feature activations.

🟡 **A Hidden Semantic Bottleneck in Conditional Embeddings of Diffusion Transformers** (arXiv:2602.21596, ICLR 2026): Conditional embeddings in DiTs show extreme angular similarity (>99%) and semantic information concentrates in a small subset of dimensions. Pruning 2/3 of embedding dimensions preserves or improves generation quality.

children.title: Semantic Bottleneck & SAE Sparsity

children.content: This directly supports the premise behind all four ideas: diffusion models already learn naturally sparse, low-dimensional conditional representations. If semantic info concentrates in few dimensions, SAE decomposition should be particularly effective — SAEs are designed to find exactly these sparse, meaningful directions. For Idea 1, this suggests the structured latent space may need fewer SAE features than expected. For Idea 3 (noise schedules), the 'head' vs 'tail' dimension distinction maps to destroying low-information dimensions first.

🟢 **Simple Denoising Diffusion Language Models** (arXiv:2510.22926, Oct 2025, updated Feb 2026): Simplifies Uniform State Diffusion Model (USDM) training with a denoising-only loss that optimizes only noise-replaced tokens, plus a regularization term. Scales to larger models. Infrastructure paper supporting Idea 4 — simpler training losses make it easier to add SAE-guided constraints.

children.title: 📡 Feb 27, 2026: Sampler Correctness, Reward Stitching & Conditional Estimators
[Feb 27] ⚠️ Few-Step Discrete Diffusion Samplers Are Distributionally Incorrect (Feb 23, 2026)

children: Paper:
Tang et al., 'Is Your Diffusion Sampler Actually Correct? A Sampler-Centric Evaluation of Discrete Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2602.19619, Feb 23, 2026)
Key insight:

Introduces an oracle evaluation framework using exact HMM posteriors to isolate sampler-induced error from denoiser approximation error. CRITICAL FINDING: few-step discrete diffusion samplers are NOT distributionally correct even under an ORACLE denoiser — transition-level mismatch vanishes only as steps approach sequence length. Moreover, improvements in NLL, generative perplexity, or MAUVE do NOT imply correct sampling. LLaDA shows persistent deviation and entropy collapse even with many steps.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ IMPORTANT CAVEAT for all ideas. If few-step sampling is fundamentally flawed, SAE-guided interventions at each step interact with an already-incorrect sampling process. This means: (1) for Idea 4 (dictionary-guided denoising), SAE corrections might partially compensate for sampler error — or exacerbate it. (2) for Idea 3 (semantic schedules), the noise schedule interacts with sampler correctness in ways we need to understand. (3) Practical implication: SAE-guided diffusion may need MORE steps than standard to maintain distributional accuracy, offsetting efficiency gains. The LLaDA entropy collapse is especially concerning — it suggests the model converges too aggressively, which SAE guidance could either help (by maintaining diversity through feature steering) or hurt (by adding another convergence pressure).

children.title: [Feb 27] 🔥 Reward-Guided Stitching: Step-Level Recombination of Diffusion Reasoning (Feb 26, 2026)

children: Paper:
Miles et al., 'Test-Time Scaling with Diffusion Language Models via Reward-Guided Stitching' (arXiv:2602.22871, Feb 26, 2026)
Key insight:

Proposes 'Stitching Noisy Diffusion Thoughts' — samples many diverse, low-cost reasoning trajectories from a masked DLM, scores every intermediate step with a process reward model (PRM), then stitches highest-quality steps ACROSS trajectories into a composite rationale. An AR solver then recomputes the final answer from the stitched rationale. Training-free, +23.8% average accuracy across 6 math/coding tasks, 1.8x latency reduction vs Dream/LLaDA. Code released.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ ARCHITECTURE FOR IDEAS 2 & 4. The step-level scoring and stitching framework is a natural fit for SAE-based guidance: (1) Replace the PRM with SAE feature coherence scoring — score each intermediate step by how well its SAE features align with target semantics. (2) The cross-trajectory stitching enables composing best semantic features from different generation attempts. (3) The modular pipeline (diffusion explores, scorer evaluates, AR solver refines) maps to: diffusion generates diverse candidates → SAE features evaluate semantic quality → solver produces final output. The finding that low-confidence diffusion sampling produces more diverse, useful candidates is interesting — it means SAE-guided exploration (deliberately sampling diverse feature configurations) could improve final quality.

children.title: [Feb 27] Sample-Efficient Discrete Diffusion via Round-Robin Conditionals (Feb 24, 2026)

children: Paper:
'Discrete Diffusion with Sample-Efficient Estimators for Conditionals' (arXiv:2602.20293, Feb 24, 2026)
Key insight:

Instead of approximating a discrete score function, directly estimates single-site conditionals using sample-efficient estimators combined with round-robin noising/denoising dynamics. Preserves combinatorial structure of discrete data while allowing tractable inference. A principled alternative to score-based approaches like SEDD.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ THEORETICAL ALTERNATIVE for Idea 4. The single-site conditional estimation approach could be combined with SAE-guided constraints: at each site (token position), the conditional estimator could be augmented with SAE feature information about what that position 'should be' given the current global feature state. The round-robin dynamics (visiting one site at a time) are also interesting for SAE integration — you could check SAE feature coherence after each single-site update, enabling very fine-grained dictionary-guided denoising.

children.title: 📡 Mar 2, 2026: Temporal SAEs — Sequential Coherence for Interpretable Features

children.text: One major find this sweep: Temporal SAEs (T-SAEs) from ICLR 2026 (oral). Updated v2 dropped Feb 25. This directly addresses a core challenge for our SAE+diffusion approach.

children.title: 🔥🔥🔥 Temporal Sparse Autoencoders: Sequential Coherence Without Supervision (arXiv:2511.05541, ICLR 2026 Oral)

children.text: Oesterling et al. introduce T-SAEs that add a contrastive loss encouraging consistent feature activations over adjacent tokens. Standard SAEs treat each token as i.i.d., which produces noisy, token-specific features. T-SAEs recover smoother, more coherent semantic concepts — and crucially, they disentangle semantic from syntactic features in a self-supervised manner. No sacrifice in reconstruction quality.

Why this matters for us: This is a DIRECT upgrade for all four of our SAE+diffusion ideas. (1) Structured Latent Spaces: T-SAE features are temporally coherent, meaning diffusion in T-SAE space would produce smoother denoising trajectories — features wouldn't flicker on/off token-by-token. (2) Feature Steering: semantic/syntactic disentanglement means we can steer high-level meaning while letting syntax resolve naturally during denoising. (3) Semantically-Aware Noise Schedules: T-SAEs literally give us the hierarchy we need — semantic features (smooth, high-level) destroyed last, syntactic features (local, token-specific) destroyed first. (4) Dictionary-Guided Denoising: coherent features make better 'parts lists' for projection steps.

The paper also notes standard SAEs 'lack utility for steering' (citing Bhalla et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025) — T-SAEs may fix exactly this. If the features are temporally coherent, activating a feature during generation should produce coherent *passages*, not just token-level effects. This could be the missing piece for Idea 2.

children.title: 📡 Mar 1, 2026: Ψ-Samplers Challenge Masked Diffusion Dominance

children.text: One new paper this sweep. The field is consolidating around infrastructure and sampling quality — good sign for building on top.

children.title: The Diffusion Duality Ch. II: Ψ-Samplers (arXiv:2602.21185)

children.text: Sahoo et al. introduce Predictor-Corrector samplers for uniform-state discrete diffusion (Duo) that generalize prior methods. Key result: uniform-state diffusion + PC sampling outperforms masked diffusion (MDLM) on both language and image modeling, and crucially keeps improving with more steps (unlike ancestral sampling which plateaus). Also: 25% training time reduction and 33% memory savings via curriculum.

Relevance to our ideas: Uniform-state diffusion's self-correction ability is exactly what SAE-guided generation needs — the ability to course-correct toward target features mid-denoising. If masked diffusion can't self-correct (tokens once unmasked are fixed), that's a fundamental limitation for Idea 2 (feature steering). This paper argues uniform-state is the better substrate for guided generation. Connects to the Sampler Correctness paper (2602.19619) — PC sampling may fix the distributional correctness issues that plague few-step samplers.

children.title: 📡 Mar 3, 2026: NAP Parallel Decoding & DLM Survey

children: Two notable finds this sweep:
1. NAP: Non-Autoregressive Parallel DLMs

(Li et al., Feb 26, arXiv:2602.23225): Diagnoses why DLMs converge to AR-like decoding — mismatch between DLM objectives and sequential training data. Proposes NAP: curate training data as multiple independent reasoning trajectories + parallel-forced decoding. Gains grow with parallelism.
For us:

Data-centric fix for parallel generation aligns with our SAE-guided approach — SAE features could provide the 'independent trajectory' structure NAP needs. Our recursive midpoint idea (Entry 77) naturally decomposes into parallel-friendly subtrees.
2. 'A Survey on Diffusion Language Models'

(VILA-Lab, arXiv:2508.10875, Aug 2025 but updated): Comprehensive survey covering MDLM, SEDD, LLaDA, Dream-7B, DiffuGPT/DiffuLLaMA. Dream-7B (from Qwen 2.5 7B) outperforms both LLaDA-8B and LLaMA3-8B. Useful reference for positioning our work.
Also noted:

AR-MAP (2602.02178, Feb 2026) — AR LLMs as implicit teachers for DLMs. Already in Awesome-DLMs list. Steering Diffusion Transformers with SAEs (OpenReview, Oct 2025) — multi-layer SAE steering for image diffusion, not text. Tangential.

children.title: Next Steps

children: 🔥 PRIORITY: Study DLM-Scope codebase — they already have SAEs trained on LLaDA/Dream. This is our starting point.
🧪 Replicate DLM-Scope's finding that SAE insertion reduces loss in early layers of DLMs. Understand WHY.
📐 Experiment: Compare SAE-based steering in DLMs (DLM-Scope approach) vs. LLMs. Quantify the improvement.
🔍 Investigate GLP as complementary approach: can GLP's manifold projection improve SAE-guided generation?
📊 Design SAE-feature-aware noise schedule using LoMDM's learnable ordering framework.
📅 Prototype review: Feb 20, 2025 — update based on DLM-Scope findings

children.title: 📡 Feb 28, 2026: One-Step SAEs, Discrete Diffusion Survey & Scaling to 10B
[Feb 28] 🔥🔥 One-Step is Enough: SAEs for T2I Diffusion Models (NeurIPS 2025)

children: Paper:
'One-Step is Enough: Sparse Autoencoders for Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2410.22366, NeurIPS 2025 poster)
Key insight:

Training SAEs on diffusion model activations does NOT require collecting activations across all denoising steps — a SINGLE timestep's activations suffice for learning interpretable, steerable features. This dramatically reduces the cost of SAE training for diffusion models. Demonstrates decomposition of intermediate representations into sparse sums of interpretable features that enable fine-grained control.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ CRITICAL PRACTICAL FINDING for all four ideas. The biggest practical concern with SAE+diffusion is cost — if you need to train SAEs across every denoising timestep, it's prohibitive. This paper eliminates that concern: one-step training produces features that generalize across timesteps. For Idea 1 (structured latent spaces), this means SAE feature space is consistent across the denoising trajectory. For Idea 3 (semantic noise schedules), the timestep-invariance of SAE features validates that the same feature vocabulary can guide scheduling at any noise level. For Idea 4 (dictionary-guided denoising), dictionary features learned from one timestep can be applied at every step.
Connection to DLM-Scope:

DLM-Scope trains SAEs on text DLMs; this paper trains SAEs on image diffusion. Together they confirm SAEs work on both modalities. The 'one-step sufficiency' finding should transfer to text DLMs, making our SAE training pipeline much simpler.

children.title: [Feb 28] Discrete Diffusion in LLMs & MLLMs: Comprehensive Survey (Jun 2025)

children: Paper:
'Discrete Diffusion in Large Language and Multimodal Models: A Survey' (arXiv:2506.13759, Jun 2025)
Key contribution:

First systematic survey of discrete diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) and multimodal LLMs (dMLLMs). Covers the shift from AR models to multi-token, parallel decoding via full attention and denoising-based generation. Catalogs architectures, training paradigms, and scaling approaches across the field.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ REFERENCE RESOURCE. Provides the taxonomic framework for positioning our SAE+diffusion work within the broader landscape. Useful for literature review and identifying which diffusion families are most amenable to SAE integration.

children.title: [Feb 28] Scaling Discrete Diffusion to 10B Parameters Confirmed (ICLR 2026 submission)

children: Paper:
von Rütte et al., 'Scaling Behavior of Discrete Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2512.10858, updated Feb 2026)
Update:

Now confirmed as the largest publicly known uniform diffusion model at 10B parameters trained for 10²² FLOPs. Reformulates the discrete diffusion ELBO in terms of signal-to-noise ratio, closing the gap to continuous diffusion theory. Training code and models to be open-sourced. The SNR reformulation simplifies both theory and implementation — relevant for Idea 3 (noise schedules could be expressed in SNR terms).
Summary:

The 'One-Step is Enough' finding is the most impactful new result — it removes a major practical barrier to SAE+diffusion integration. The field continues to mature rapidly with comprehensive surveys and scaling studies confirming discrete diffusion as a viable paradigm.

children.title: [Mar 1] 🔥🔥🔥 Steerling-8B: First Inherently Interpretable Diffusion LM with Concept Routing (Feb 23, 2026)

children: Source:
Guide Labs, 'Steerling-8B' (guidelabs.ai, Feb 23, 2026). Model: HuggingFace guidelabs/steerling-8b
Key insight:

An 8B-parameter causal discrete diffusion LM built to be interpretable BY CONSTRUCTION. Decomposes embeddings into three explicit pathways: ~33K supervised 'known' concepts, ~100K 'discovered' concepts, and a residual. Concepts route through linear paths with per-concept contribution decomposition. 84%+ of token-level contribution flows through the concept module. Trained on 1.35T tokens, competitive with models using 2-7x more compute.
Capabilities:

(1) Suppress or amplify specific concepts at inference without retraining, (2) training data provenance for any output chunk, (3) inference-time alignment via concept control replacing thousands of safety examples. Every generated token traces to input context, concepts, and training data.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ THIS IS OUR IDEA 1 IMPLEMENTED AT SCALE! Steerling-8B is essentially a diffusion LM with a built-in SAE-like concept bottleneck. Their ~33K supervised + ~100K discovered concepts ARE the structured latent space we proposed. The linear concept-to-logit path ensures decomposability — exactly what SAE features provide. The 84% concept routing means the model doesn't bypass the interpretable pathway. Key difference from our proposal: they train concepts INTO the model architecture rather than extracting them post-hoc via SAEs. This validates the thesis but suggests the most effective approach may be architectural integration rather than post-hoc SAE application.
New questions:

(1) How do Steerling's learned concepts compare to SAE features from DLM-Scope? (2) Could we use SAEs to analyze Steerling's residual pathway (the 16% not routed through concepts)? (3) Is architectural concept integration always better than post-hoc SAE extraction, or do they capture different things?

children.title: [Mar 1] dLLMs Exploit Asymmetry for Planning — Mechanistic Analysis (Feb 23, 2026)

children: Paper:
'Discrete Diffusion Models Exploit Asymmetry to Solve Lookahead Planning Tasks' (arXiv:2602.19980, Feb 23, 2026)
Key insight:

NAR diffusion models solve planning by exploiting a critical asymmetry: forward generation requires complex lookahead at branching junctions, but reverse generation is often deterministic. dLLMs learn to use future tokens to decode BACKWARDS, avoiding the need to learn complex traversal. Result: perfect accuracy with exponentially fewer training examples and shallower architectures than AR models.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ MECHANISTIC INSIGHT for all ideas. This explains WHY diffusion LMs might be better targets for SAE-guided generation than AR LMs: their bidirectional access enables backward decoding from semantic targets. SAE features specifying desired semantics could serve as the 'future tokens' that guide backward planning. Complements the Mechanism Shift paper — another piece of evidence that dLLMs compute fundamentally differently from AR models.

children.title: [Mar 1] IDLM: 4x-64x Faster DLM Inference via Inverse Distillation (Feb 22, 2026)

children: Paper:
'IDLM: Inverse-distilled Diffusion Language Models' (arXiv:2602.19066, Feb 22, 2026)
Key insight:

Extends inverse distillation from continuous to discrete diffusion, proving unique solution existence for the inverse formulation. Introduces gradient-stable relaxations for discrete backpropagation. Achieves 4x-64x step reduction while preserving teacher entropy and generative perplexity.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ PRACTICAL ENABLER. Fewer denoising steps means SAE-guided interventions per step become less costly. Combined with DSL's 4x reduction, total overhead for SAE guidance could be minimal. The gradient-stable relaxations for discrete backprop could also help train SAE-guided denoising objectives.

children.title: [Mar 1] dLLM Framework: Unified Open-Source Toolkit for Diffusion LMs (Feb 26, 2026)

children: Paper:
'dLLM: Simple Diffusion Language Modeling' (arXiv:2602.22661, Feb 26, 2026)
Key insight:

Unified open-source framework standardizing DLM training, inference, and evaluation. Supports LLaDA, Dream, MDLM, BD3LM. Includes recipes for converting BERT or AR models into DLMs with accessible compute. Code: github.com/ZHZisZZ/dllm
Relevance:

⭐⭐ PRACTICAL INFRASTRUCTURE. A unified framework for DLM experimentation dramatically lowers the barrier to prototyping SAE+diffusion ideas. Could serve as the base for our experimental work — add SAE hooks to dLLM's training/inference pipeline.

children.title: [Mar 1] DLM Sampler Correctness: Metrics Don't Imply Correct Sampling (Feb 23, 2026)

children: Paper:
'Is Your Diffusion Sampler Actually Correct?' (arXiv:2602.19619, Feb 23, 2026)
Key insight:

Uses an oracle HMM framework to isolate sampler-induced error from denoiser error. Finding: few-step discrete diffusion samplers are NOT distributionally correct even with perfect denoisers. Mismatch only vanishes as steps approach sequence length. Improvements in NLL/perplexity/MAUVE don't imply correct sampling.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ IMPORTANT CAVEAT. If standard samplers aren't correct, SAE-guided interventions during sampling could either help (correcting distributional drift) or compound errors. The oracle framework could be used to evaluate whether SAE guidance improves distributional correctness. Also suggests that step-reduction techniques (IDLM, DSL) need careful evaluation beyond standard metrics.
Mar 1 Summary:

Steerling-8B is the headline: the first diffusion LM with built-in interpretable concept routing at scale. This validates our core thesis (Idea 1) that structured concept spaces work for diffusion text generation. The field is also maturing rapidly with unified tooling (dLLM), faster inference (IDLM), and deeper mechanistic understanding (asymmetry exploitation, sampler correctness).

children.title: 🔬 March 3, 2026: TADA — SAE Steering Comes to Diffusion Models
TADA! SAE-Based Activation Steering of Audio Diffusion (Feb 12, 2026)

children: Paper:
'TADA! Tuning Audio Diffusion Models through Activation Steering' (Staniszewski et al., arXiv:2602.11910, Feb 12, 2026)
Key insight:

First paper applying SAE-based activation steering directly to diffusion model internals. Uses both Contrastive Activation Addition and Sparse Autoencoders on audio diffusion models. Finds that semantic musical concepts (instruments, vocals, genre) are controlled by a small, shared subset of attention layers. SAE steering in those specific layers enables precise control over tempo, mood, and instrumentation.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT VALIDATION of our Idea 2 (SAE feature steering for conditional generation) — but in audio, not text. Proves that SAE features are interpretable and steerable within diffusion architectures, not just autoregressive LLMs. Key architectural insight: steering works best in specific attention layers, not uniformly — matching what we know from Arad et al.'s input/output feature distinction. The jump from audio diffusion → text diffusion is non-trivial but the core mechanism (SAE features in attention layers of denoising networks) should transfer.
Mar 3 Summary:

TADA bridges the gap between SAE interpretability and diffusion generation — the exact intersection we're exploring. While it's audio (not text), it's the first evidence that SAE-based steering works inside diffusion model internals during generation. Combined with DLM-Scope (SAEs work better on diffusion LMs than AR LLMs) and Steerling-8B (concept routing in diffusion LMs), the pieces for SAE-guided text diffusion are falling into place.

children.title: 🔬 March 4, 2026: CoDAR — Continuous Diffusion Closes the Gap
CoDAR: Token Rounding Is the Bottleneck for Continuous Diffusion (Mar 3, 2026)

children: Paper:
Shen et al., 'CoDAR: Continuous Diffusion Language Models are More Powerful Than You Think' (arXiv:2603.02547, Mar 3, 2026)
Key insight:

Through controlled token-recovery experiments, identifies TOKEN ROUNDING (the final projection from denoised embeddings to discrete tokens) as the primary bottleneck preventing continuous diffusion LMs from matching discrete approaches. Proposes a two-stage fix: keep diffusion entirely continuous in embedding space, then use a context-conditional AR decoder that cross-attends to denoised embeddings for contextualized rounding. Becomes competitive with strong discrete DLMs on LM1B and OpenWebText.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ DIRECT INSIGHT FOR IDEA 1. The rounding bottleneck is exactly the problem SAE features could help solve. SAE feature activations are continuous but semantically structured — they provide a RICHER intermediate representation than raw embeddings for the rounding step. Instead of CoDAR's generic contextual decoder, SAE features could serve as an interpretable bridge between continuous diffusion and discrete tokens: diffuse in SAE space → SAE features naturally decompose into semantic components → each component maps to token distributions more cleanly than raw embeddings. Also reinforces FLM's finding that continuous diffusion for text is viable when the discretization step is handled properly.
New question:

Could SAE feature space serve as a 'structured rounding' mechanism? If SAE features decompose embeddings into interpretable dimensions, projecting denoised embeddings through the SAE before rounding could improve token recovery — combining CoDAR's insight with our Idea 1.

children.title: GSAE: Graph-Regularized SAEs Double Steering Effectiveness (Dec 2025)

children: Paper:

Sun et al., 'GSAE: Graph-Regularized Sparse Autoencoders for Robust LLM Safety Steering' (arXiv:2512.06655, Dec 2025). See also: Breckner et al., 'Diffusion LLMs can think EoS-by-EoS' (arXiv:2603.05197, Mar 5 2026) — shows diffusion LLMs use EoS token representations as hidden scratchpads for reasoning, with causal interventions confirming information content. Highly relevant to understanding how SAE features could be steered at the EoS-token level during diffusion denoising.
Key insight:

Standard SAE features lack semantic clustering — related concepts scatter across the feature space. GSAE adds graph regularization during SAE training: semantically related features are encouraged to cluster, while unrelated features are pushed apart. Result: GSAE steering achieves 82% selective refusal rate vs 42% for standard SAE steering, while maintaining strong task accuracy (70% TriviaQA, 65% TruthfulQA, 74% GSM8K). The graph structure is derived from feature co-activation patterns.
Relevance:

⭐⭐ QUALITY IMPROVEMENT FOR ALL IDEAS. If we use SAE features as a diffusion latent space (Idea 1) or for steering (Idea 2), the quality of SAE feature structure matters enormously. GSAE's graph regularization nearly doubles steering effectiveness — suggesting that for SAE+diffusion, we should use graph-regularized SAEs rather than vanilla ones. The co-activation graph captures feature interdependencies that a diffusion model could exploit: features that co-activate form natural 'modes' in the latent space. For Idea 3 (semantic noise schedules), the graph structure could define which feature clusters to noise/denoise together.
New question:

Could the GSAE co-activation graph serve as the structure for a graph-based diffusion process over SAE features? Graph diffusion (denoising on a graph) is well-studied — combining it with SAE feature graphs could yield a principled architecture for our Idea 1.
Mar 4 Summary:

CoDAR identifies the specific failure mode of continuous text diffusion (token rounding) and fixes it, making continuous diffusion competitive with discrete. GSAE shows that graph-regularized SAEs nearly double steering effectiveness over vanilla SAEs — suggesting that SAE feature quality is a key lever for our SAE+diffusion ideas. Together: the field is converging on continuous representations for text, and structured (graph-regularized) SAE features could provide the optimal bridge between continuous diffusion and discrete tokens.

children.title: Mar 6, 2026: Diffusion LLMs Think in EoS Tokens

children: New finding:

Breckner et al. show that diffusion LLMs (LLaDA 1.5/2.0, Dream-v0) use EoS token representations as a hidden scratchpad for reasoning. When generation length exceeds what's needed for the answer, the model pads with EoS tokens — and these carry critical problem-solving information. Patching EoS hidden states from a counterfactual generation changes the output, confirming causal influence.

children.title: [Mar 6] 🔥 Diffusion LLMs Can Think EoS-by-EoS (Mar 5, 2026)

children: Paper:
Breckner et al., 'Diffusion LLMs can think EoS-by-EoS' (arXiv:2603.05197, Mar 5 2026)
Key insight:

In diffusion LLMs, EoS tokens aren't just padding — they serve as computational workspace. The model uses their hidden representations to solve harder reasoning problems (addition, entity tracking, Sudoku). This is a diffusion-specific form of 'thinking' that has no autoregressive analog.
Relevance:

⭐⭐⭐ MAJOR for Ideas 1 & 2. If diffusion LLMs use EoS positions for hidden computation, then SAE features extracted from EoS positions during denoising could reveal the model's 'reasoning trace' — what it's working out before committing to an answer. This opens a new steering vector: instead of steering the content tokens, steer the EoS scratchpad to guide reasoning direction. For our structured latent space idea, the EoS positions represent a natural location for SAE-decomposed reasoning features. For DLM-Scope's finding that SAEs work better on diffusion LMs, EoS scratchpad representations may be WHY — the model concentrates structured reasoning information there.
New question:

Can we train SAEs specifically on EoS-position hidden states from diffusion LLMs to extract interpretable 'reasoning features'? If so, steering those features could directly control the model's reasoning process — a much more powerful form of SAE-guided generation than steering content tokens.
Mar 6 Summary:

The EoS-as-scratchpad finding is potentially the most important result for our SAE+diffusion program since DLM-Scope. It identifies WHERE diffusion LLMs do their reasoning (EoS tokens), which tells us WHERE to point SAE probes for maximum interpretability. Field now at 44 tracked papers.
